title: Project Scaffolding + esbuild Validation
description:
Problem: The knowledge base CLI requires a build pipeline. The project currently has no npm dependencies or build tooling. esbuild bundling with Orama + MsgPack is the highest implementation risk per the design doc — if it doesn't work, the entire architecture needs rethinking. This must be validated first.

Solution: Create project scaffolding (package.json, directory structure) and validate that esbuild bundles Orama and MsgPack into a single CJS file that runs correctly.

Outcome: A working build pipeline producing skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs from src/knowledge/index.js. Design doc estimates ~110-120KB minified; threshold set at 150KB to allow headroom for application code while catching unexpected bloat.

Do:
- Create package.json at project root with devDependencies: @orama/orama, @msgpack/msgpack, esbuild. Pin exact versions to avoid API drift (design doc: verify exact API shape against targeted Orama version at implementation time).
- Add to .gitignore: node_modules/, .workflows/.knowledge/store.msp
- Create directory structure:
  - src/knowledge/ (source files — NOT copied by AGNTC, lives outside skills/)
  - build/ (build config — NOT copied by AGNTC)
  - skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/ (bundled output destination — IS copied by AGNTC)
  - skills/workflow-knowledge/chunking/ (phase configs directory, populated in Phase 2)
- Create build/knowledge.build.js — esbuild config using: --bundle --platform=node --format=cjs --minify. Entry: src/knowledge/index.js. Output: skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs
- Create src/knowledge/index.js as the entry point that will grow into the full knowledge CLI. Structure it as a module with exports (not a throwaway script). For now it should import @orama/orama (create, insert, search) and @msgpack/msgpack (encode, decode), perform a trivial round-trip with each library, and exit cleanly. Both imports must be actively used — esbuild tree-shaking will remove unused imports. Subsequent tasks in this phase will replace the trivial operations with real store, provider, and persistence code.
- Add a build script to package.json: node build/knowledge.build.js
- The bundled knowledge.cjs MUST be committed to the repo (not gitignored). AGNTC installs from git tags with no build step — the bundle must be present in the tree at tag time. During development, rebuild and commit the bundle when source changes.
- Verify __dirname in bundled output resolves to the script directory. esbuild preserves __dirname as-is in --platform=node --format=cjs output (verified in design doc). The knowledge CLI uses this at runtime to find chunking configs via path.join(__dirname, '..', 'chunking').
- Plain JS, not TypeScript — matches the existing manifest.cjs convention.
- Minimum Node 18 required by Orama. Node 20+ recommended (Node 18 fetch emits ExperimentalWarning to stderr — cosmetic, not functional).

Acceptance Criteria:
- npm install && npm run build succeeds without errors
- Bundle exists at skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs and is committed to git
- Bundle size is under 150KB (design doc estimates ~110-120KB minified — 150KB provides ~25% headroom for growth across Phase 1 tasks while catching unexpected bloat)
- node skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs runs and exits cleanly
- __dirname in the bundle resolves to skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/, not src/knowledge/
- node_modules/ and .workflows/.knowledge/store.msp are in .gitignore (but knowledge.cjs is NOT)
- src/knowledge/index.js is structured as a module with exports, not a disposable script

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-build.sh (shell test following existing migration test conventions — PASS/FAIL counters, assert_eq function, summary line)
- it builds without errors (npm run build exits 0)
- it produces a bundle under 150KB (stat the output file)
- it runs and exits with code 0 (node knowledge.cjs)
- __dirname resolves to the script directory, not the source directory (run the bundle, capture stdout, assert path contains skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts)

Edge Cases:
- esbuild tree-shaking removes imports that appear unused — the minimal index.js must actively use both libraries in its trivial operations
- If bundle exceeds 150KB at Task 1-1 (trivial code), investigate — libraries alone are ~90KB per design doc, so Task 1-1's minimal bundle should be ~90-100KB. Significant deviation means esbuild is including more than expected.
- Both @orama/orama and @msgpack/msgpack are zero-dependency pure JS — no native modules, no node-gyp. If npm install fails, it is not a dependency issue.
- NOTE: later Phase 1 tasks (1-3, 1-4, 1-5) add real store/persistence/locking code and the bundle will grow. If growth pushes past 150KB during Phase 1, re-evaluate the threshold — do not silently raise it without investigating what's being included.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Build & Distribution section (bundle estimated at ~110-120KB minified, line 208 and 446) and Runtime config file discovery subsection (line 461)

title: Embedding Provider Interface + StubProvider
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs an embedding provider to convert text into vectors for semantic search. All subsequent tasks in this phase (store, persistence, integration test) depend on having a provider interface to program against. The real OpenAI provider comes in Phase 4, but testing needs a deterministic provider now that produces consistent vectors without API calls.

Solution: Define the KnowledgeProvider interface and implement a StubProvider that generates deterministic vectors by hashing input text.

Outcome: A provider interface that the store, CLI, and all tests program against, plus a StubProvider that enables the entire test suite without external dependencies.

TERMINOLOGY DISAMBIGUATION (important — the design doc uses two similar terms):
- StubProvider (this task) — a TEST provider that returns deterministic fake vectors via hash. ALWAYS returns vectors. Configured via provider: stub in config. Used for development and testing only. NOT a production mode.
- Stub mode / keyword-only mode (design doc lines 322-331) — a PRODUCTION degraded mode triggered when NO provider is configured or no API key is available. Stores documents WITHOUT vectors (omits the vector field). Uses BM25 fulltext search only. Triggered by the ABSENCE of a provider, not by provider: stub.
These are DIFFERENT concepts that share a similar name. StubProvider is a first-class provider implementation for testing. Stub mode is the absence of any provider in production. Do not conflate them.

Do:
- Create src/knowledge/embeddings.js exporting the interface contract and StubProvider implementation.
- Define KnowledgeProvider interface with four methods/accessors:
  - embed(text) -> vector (array of floats, length matching dimensions())
  - embedBatch(texts[]) -> vectors[] (array of vectors, one per input)
  - dimensions() -> number (the vector dimensionality this provider produces)
  - model() -> string (a stable identifier for this provider's model, used by Task 3-3 to write into metadata.json and by Task 3-4 / Task 4-6 for mismatch detection). Every concrete provider must return a stable, non-empty string.
- Implement StubProvider:
  - Constructor accepts optional dimensions parameter. Implementer picks a sensible default (e.g., 128 for smaller/faster tests, or 1536 to match production). Downstream tasks must not assume a specific default.
  - embed(text): hash the input string deterministically to produce a float vector of length dimensions(). Does not need to be cryptographically strong — consistency and differentiation matter, not quality.
  - embedBatch(texts): maps over embed() for each input. No batching optimisation needed — this is for testing.
  - dimensions(): returns the configured dimension count.
  - model(): returns a stable identifier string for the stub provider (e.g., "stub"). This value is written to metadata.json by Task 3-3 and compared on mismatch checks — it must never be null, undefined, or empty.
  - CRITICAL: never return null for vectors. Orama crashes when a vector field is null. Return a real vector array from embed/embedBatch. Production keyword-only mode (no provider configured at all) is handled at the calling layer by omitting the vector field — that is NOT StubProvider's responsibility. StubProvider is a real provider that always returns vectors.
  - Same input text MUST always produce the same vector (deterministic).
  - Different input text MUST produce different vectors (distinguishable).
- The provider dimensions and the Orama schema dimensions must match. Task 1-3 (store creation) will read dimensions from the provider. Production uses whatever the OpenAI provider (Phase 4) exposes — typically 1536 for text-embedding-3-small.
- Rebuild after implementation: npm run build to verify embeddings.js bundles into knowledge.cjs correctly.

Acceptance Criteria:
- embed('hello') returns an array of floats with length equal to dimensions()
- embed('hello') called twice returns identical vectors
- embed('hello') and embed('world') return different vectors
- embedBatch(['a', 'b', 'c']) returns exactly 3 vectors, each with correct dimensions
- dimensions() returns the configured value
- model() returns a stable, non-empty string
- embed() never returns null or undefined — always a real array
- Module exports both the StubProvider class and a clear description of the interface contract (JSDoc or comments documenting what providers must implement, including the model() requirement)
- JSDoc/comments explicitly note that StubProvider is for testing, and that production keyword-only mode is a separate concept (no provider configured, handled by the caller)
- npm run build succeeds with the new module included

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-embeddings.cjs (Node test using node:test module and assert, following existing discovery test conventions — describe/it blocks, beforeEach/afterEach if needed)
- it returns a vector of correct length for a single string
- it returns identical vectors for identical input (determinism)
- it returns different vectors for different input (differentiation)
- it handles embedBatch with multiple inputs correctly
- it respects custom dimensions parameter
- it returns a stable non-empty model() identifier
- it never returns null (explicit assertion)
- it handles empty string input without error
- it handles very long string input without error

Edge Cases:
- Empty string input — must still return a valid vector, not crash
- Very long input — must produce a vector of correct length regardless of input size
- Unicode input — must handle non-ASCII characters
- embedBatch with empty array — should return empty array
- embedBatch with single item — should return array of one vector, identical to calling embed() directly

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Embedding Provider section (KnowledgeProvider interface, embed/embedBatch/dimensions methods), Testing Strategy section (StubProvider description, mock provider usage), Knowledge Base is Required Infrastructure section (lines 322-331, stub mode / keyword-only as the distinct production concept), Provider mismatch detection finding #10 (line 982, requires provider/model/dimensions stored in metadata.json — every provider must expose all three)

title: Orama Store — Creation, Insert, Remove, Fulltext Search, Filtering
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs a store to hold chunked workflow artifacts with structured metadata. This is the first task using the Orama library directly — the API surface needs to be validated against the pinned version. Vector and hybrid search are deferred to Task 1-4; this task establishes creation, insertion, removal, and fulltext capabilities. The store must also support vectorless documents for keyword-only production mode (where no embedding provider is configured).

Solution: Implement a store module wrapping Orama with the schema from the design doc, supporting document insertion with all metadata fields, removal by identity key, and BM25 fulltext search with enum-based metadata filtering. The embedding field is optional at insert time to support keyword-only mode.

Outcome: A store module that can create an Orama instance, insert documents with or without vectors, remove documents by identity key, search by text content, and filter results by metadata — the foundation that Tasks 1-4 and 1-5 build on.

Do:
- Create src/knowledge/store.js exporting store functions. This module will grow across Tasks 1-3 and 1-4 — structure it to accommodate future additions (vector search, hybrid search, save/load) without restructuring.
- Create the Orama store with this schema (from design doc):
    id: 'string'
    content: 'string' (full text of the chunk — verbatim from artifact)
    work_unit: 'enum'
    work_type: 'enum' (epic, feature, bugfix, quick-fix, cross-cutting)
    phase: 'enum' (research, discussion, investigation, specification)
    topic: 'enum'
    confidence: 'enum' (low, low-medium, medium, high)
    source_file: 'string'
    timestamp: 'number' (epoch ms)
    embedding: 'vector[N]' where N = dimensions parameter
- The dimensions parameter must be passed in at store creation time. It comes from the embedding provider's dimensions() method. For tests using StubProvider (Task 1-2), this will be 128. For production with OpenAI (Phase 4), this will be 1536. In keyword-only production mode (no provider), use a default (e.g., 1536) for the schema — documents will simply omit the vector field, not insert null.
- Orama enum fields are open — any string value can be inserted, no upfront declaration needed. They use Map internally for fast exact-match filtering. Verify this against the pinned Orama version.
- Implement createStore(dimensions) -> returns an Orama database instance.
- Implement insertDocument(db, doc) -> inserts a single document. The doc must include all non-vector fields: id, content, work_unit, work_type, phase, topic, confidence, source_file, timestamp. The embedding field is OPTIONAL — include it when a provider is available (returns a vector), OMIT it entirely for keyword-only mode. NEVER pass null for embedding — Orama crashes on null. Pass undefined or simply omit the property.
- Per design doc review finding #7: Orama accepts documents without the vector field when the schema defines vector[N]. Fulltext search works perfectly on vectorless docs. This task must validate that behaviour against the pinned Orama version.
- Implement removeByIdentity(db, { work_unit, phase, topic }) -> removes ALL documents matching the given work_unit + phase + topic combination. This is the core mechanism for re-indexing: remove existing chunks for an identity key, then insert fresh ones. The identity key is work_unit + phase + topic (not the document id). Use Orama's search with where clause to find matching document IDs, then remove them. Verify the exact Orama API for document removal against the pinned version.
- Implement searchFulltext(db, { term, where, limit }) -> returns search results. Uses Orama's fulltext search mode (BM25). The 'where' parameter maps to Orama's where clause for metadata filtering on enum fields. The 'limit' parameter caps result count (default 10).
- The where clause supports: exact match (phase: 'specification'), in-list (phase: { in: ['specification', 'discussion'] }), and combinations across multiple fields. Verify the exact Orama API for where clause syntax against the pinned version.
- Return results in a normalised shape: array of { id, content, work_unit, work_type, phase, topic, confidence, source_file, timestamp, score }. Extract these from Orama's native result format. This normalised shape is what all search functions (fulltext, vector, hybrid) will return — establish it now.
- Rebuild after implementation: npm run build.

Acceptance Criteria:
- createStore(128) produces a valid Orama instance without error
- A document with all schema fields INCLUDING embedding can be inserted and is retrievable
- A document WITHOUT the embedding field can be inserted and is retrievable (keyword-only mode support)
- Fulltext search on a vectorless document returns it correctly (no crash, no filtering by vector presence)
- Mixed store (some docs with vectors, some without) — fulltext search works across both
- Attempting to insert with embedding: null throws or is caught (Orama crash protection)
- removeByIdentity removes all documents matching a work_unit + phase + topic combination
- removeByIdentity does not affect documents with different identity keys
- After removeByIdentity, search no longer returns the removed documents
- Fulltext search for a term present in content returns matching documents ranked by BM25 score
- Fulltext search for a term not in any document returns empty results
- where clause filtering: search with { phase: 'specification' } returns only specification chunks
- where clause filtering: search with { phase: { in: ['specification', 'discussion'] } } returns both
- Combined filtering: where clause with multiple fields (e.g., phase + work_type) narrows correctly
- Multiple documents can be inserted and searched across
- Results include score and all metadata fields in the normalised shape
- npm run build succeeds with the store module included

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-store.cjs (Node test using node:test and assert. Import StubProvider from Task 1-2 for generating vectors.)
- it creates a store with specified dimensions
- it inserts a document with all fields (including vector from StubProvider)
- it inserts a document WITHOUT the embedding field (vectorless, keyword-only path)
- it finds a vectorless document via fulltext search
- it handles a mixed store (some docs with vectors, some without) via fulltext search
- it removes documents by identity key (work_unit + phase + topic)
- it does not remove documents with different identity keys
- it returns empty search results after removing all matching documents
- it finds a document by fulltext search on content
- it returns empty results for non-matching search
- it filters by single enum field (phase)
- it filters by enum field with in-list
- it filters by multiple enum fields simultaneously
- it returns results in the normalised shape with score and all metadata
- it handles inserting multiple documents and searching across them
- it handles duplicate document IDs (verify Orama behaviour — does it error or upsert?)
- it errors or guards when embedding is null (never passes null to Orama)

Edge Cases:
- Duplicate document ID on insert — Orama may error or silently overwrite. Verify and document the behaviour. The knowledge CLI will need to remove-then-insert for re-indexing, so understanding this is important.
- Insert with embedding: undefined vs missing field — both should work; both should NOT crash Orama
- Insert with embedding: null — must be prevented (either throw, or strip the field before insert)
- removeByIdentity when no documents match — must be a no-op, not an error
- removeByIdentity with partial identity (e.g., only work_unit) — this function always requires all three fields. Broader removal granularity (work_unit only, work_unit + phase) is handled by the CLI remove command in Phase 4, which will call this function or use a variant.
- Empty store search — must return empty array, not error
- Search with no where clause — must return all matching documents
- Search with where clause matching zero documents — must return empty array
- Very long content field — must insert and be searchable
- Enum field with value not seen before — must work (Orama enums are open)

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Orama schema section (exact field definitions), Example insert and Example hybrid search sections (API shape reference), Chunk Identity and Metadata section (identity key = work_unit + phase + topic, removal on re-index), Testing Strategy section (store tests include remove), Review finding #7 (lines 973-974, Orama accepts vectorless docs, never null), and the note 'verify exact API shape against targeted Orama version at implementation time'

title: Store Persistence + File Locking + Metadata
description:
Problem: The store from Task 1-3 works in-memory only — data is lost when the process exits. The knowledge base must persist between CLI invocations (each invocation loads the index, operates, saves). It also needs vector and hybrid search modes, concurrent write protection, and metadata tracking for provider mismatch detection.

Solution: Extend the store module with vector search, hybrid search, MsgPack persistence via Orama's save()/load(), file locking adapted from the manifest CLI, and a metadata.json file for index state.

Outcome: A store that supports all three search modes, persists to disk as a MsgPack binary, survives process restarts with identical results, and protects against concurrent write corruption.

Do:

VECTOR + HYBRID SEARCH (extend src/knowledge/store.js):
- Add searchVector(db, { vector, where, limit, similarity }) — vector similarity search (cosine, Orama's default). The vector parameter is a pre-computed embedding from the provider (the caller embeds the query text, then passes the vector here). The similarity parameter is the minimum similarity threshold, passed through to Orama's similarity field (design doc finding #15, default 0.8, resolved from config at the CLI layer). Returns results in the same normalised shape as searchFulltext from Task 1-3.
- Add searchHybrid(db, { term, vector, where, limit, textWeight, vectorWeight, similarity }) — combined BM25 + vector search. Default weights: text 0.4, vector 0.6 (from design doc). The similarity parameter is the minimum similarity threshold, passed through on the vector component of the hybrid search. Verify the exact Orama API for hybrid search configuration — the design doc notes 'verify exact API shape against targeted Orama version at implementation time' for hybridWeights and similarity.
- Both functions support the same where clause filtering as searchFulltext.

MSGPACK PERSISTENCE (extend src/knowledge/store.js):
- Add saveStore(db, storePath) — calls Orama save() to produce a plain JS object, serializes with MsgPack encode(), writes to storePath. Use atomic write: write to storePath + '.tmp', then fs.renameSync to storePath (same pattern as manifest.cjs writeManifestAtomic, line 69-74).
- Add loadStore(storePath) — reads file, deserializes with MsgPack decode(), calls Orama load() to reconstruct the database instance. Orama's load() reconstructs the full database (schema + documents + indices) from the saved object; no dimensions parameter is needed at the call site. If implementation reveals that Orama's targeted version requires additional info, extend the signature then.
- Store path default: .workflows/.knowledge/store.msp
- IMPORTANT round-trip detail: Orama's save() calls toJSON() internally, converting Float32Arrays to plain arrays. MsgPack serializes these plain arrays faithfully. On load(), Orama reconstructs typed arrays from the plain arrays. This is verified in the design doc but MUST be validated in the integration test (Task 1-5). If MsgPack encounters a raw Float32Array (not converted by toJSON), it may serialize differently.

FILE LOCKING (new src/knowledge/lock.js or within store.js):
- Adapt the manifest.cjs file locking pattern (skills/workflow-manifest/scripts/manifest.cjs lines 80-127). The exact pattern: exclusive file create with 'wx' flag, write PID, stale detection at 30s, timeout at 10s, 50ms busy-wait spin.
- Lock file location: .workflows/.knowledge/.lock
- Implement: acquireLock(lockPath), releaseLock(lockPath), withLock(lockPath, fn)
- WRITE operations (saveStore and any function that calls it) MUST acquire the lock. READ operations (loadStore for querying, all search functions) do NOT lock — stale reads are acceptable per design doc.
- withLock pattern: acquireLock, try { fn() } finally { releaseLock }

METADATA (new function or within store.js):
- Location: .workflows/.knowledge/metadata.json
- Schema: { provider, model, dimensions, last_indexed, pending }
- Field encoding:
    - provider: string (e.g. 'openai', 'stub') OR null for keyword-only mode
    - model: string (e.g. 'text-embedding-3-small') OR null for keyword-only mode
    - dimensions: number (e.g. 1536) OR null for keyword-only mode
    - last_indexed: ISO 8601 timestamp string (e.g. '2026-04-10T12:34:56.789Z') — single global timestamp of the most recent indexing operation
    - pending: array of { file, failed_at, error } entries (populated by Task 4-4). Empty array at creation.
- Keyword-only mode encoding: all three provider/model/dimensions fields MUST be null (not undefined, not missing — explicit null). This makes Task 4-6's stub-to-full detection logic simple: if metadata.provider is null AND current config has a provider with API key, show the upgrade note. Consistent null handling avoids ambiguity.
- Add writeMetadata(metadataPath, data) and readMetadata(metadataPath) functions.
- writeMetadata writes the full schema atomically (write to .tmp, rename). It never writes a partial schema — all fields are always present.
- readMetadata returns the parsed object. If the file is missing, throws a clear error (callers handle the absence — Task 3-3 creates it on first index).
- This task creates the functions but does NOT call them from the store operations — that wiring is in Task 3-3 (single-file index is the first caller).

- Rebuild after implementation: npm run build.

Acceptance Criteria:
- searchVector returns documents ranked by vector similarity when given a query vector
- searchVector accepts and passes through the similarity threshold parameter
- searchHybrid returns documents using combined text + vector scoring
- searchHybrid accepts and passes through the similarity threshold parameter
- Hybrid search with textWeight: 1, vectorWeight: 0 produces same ranking as searchFulltext
- Both vector and hybrid search support where clause filtering
- saveStore writes a .msp file to disk that is non-empty
- loadStore reads the .msp file and reconstructs a working Orama instance without additional schema arguments
- After save + load, searchFulltext returns identical results to before save
- After save + load, searchVector returns identical results to before save
- After save + load, document count matches (verify with Orama's count API or equivalent)
- File locking: withLock acquires lock, executes function, releases lock
- File locking: stale lock older than 30s is cleaned up automatically
- File locking: concurrent lock attempt within timeout eventually acquires after first releases
- writeMetadata writes the full schema with all 5 fields present
- writeMetadata handles keyword-only mode by writing provider/model/dimensions as null (not undefined)
- readMetadata returns the stored values accurately
- readMetadata throws a clear error when metadata.json is missing

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-store.cjs (extend the file from Task 1-3 with new describe blocks. Tests need filesystem access — use temp directories with setup/teardown, same pattern as discovery-test-utils.cjs)
- it searches by vector similarity and returns ranked results
- it applies the similarity threshold to vector search (results below the threshold are excluded)
- it searches by hybrid mode and returns combined results
- it applies the similarity threshold on the vector component of hybrid search
- it applies where clause filtering to vector search
- it applies where clause filtering to hybrid search
- it saves store to disk as MsgPack binary
- it loads store from disk and reconstructs a working instance
- it produces identical fulltext results after save/load round-trip
- it produces identical vector results after save/load round-trip
- it acquires and releases file lock
- it detects and cleans stale locks
- it writes metadata.json with all 5 fields (provider/model/dimensions/last_indexed/pending)
- it writes null for provider/model/dimensions in keyword-only mode
- it reads metadata.json correctly
- it throws a clear error when metadata.json is missing on read

Edge Cases:
- MsgPack round-trip with Float32Array — verify Orama's save() produces plain arrays (not typed arrays) before MsgPack serialization. If typed arrays leak through, MsgPack may encode them as binary buffers instead of number arrays, breaking load().
- Corrupted or truncated .msp file — loadStore should throw a clear error, not crash silently
- Missing .msp file on loadStore — should throw a clear error (not the same as 'not initialised' — that is checked by the knowledge check command in Phase 3)
- Lock file left behind by crashed process — stale detection (30s) handles this
- saveStore to a directory that does not exist — should error clearly, not create directories silently (the setup wizard in Phase 7 creates the directory)
- Keyword-only mode metadata — provider/model/dimensions all null means the store was built without embeddings. Task 4-6 uses this to detect stub-to-full upgrade opportunities.
- similarity threshold of 0 — no filtering, all vector results included. similarity of 1 — only exact matches. Both extremes should work without error.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Storage section (Orama save/load, MsgPack benchmarks, round-trip fidelity verification), Serialization benchmarks table, Concurrent access / lost writes review finding (file locking pattern), the note about Float32Array conversion in toJSON(), similarity_threshold configurability finding #15 (line 999, default 0.8, passed on every query), and Stub mode schema compatibility finding #7 (line 974, documents without vectors)

title: Store Integration Test
description:
Problem: Tasks 1-1 through 1-4 build the store layer piece by piece — scaffolding, provider, store operations, persistence. Each has its own unit tests. But no test validates that all the pieces work together end-to-end: creating a store, inserting real documents via StubProvider, removing by identity, persisting to disk, reloading, and getting identical search results across all three modes. This is the test that catches integration failures between Orama, MsgPack, and the provider — particularly the Float32Array round-trip through serialization that the design doc flags as a verified-but-critical assumption.

Solution: A single comprehensive integration test file that exercises the full Phase 1 stack as a realistic workflow: multiple documents across different phases and work units, all three search modes, removal by identity, persistence round-trip, metadata verification, and the no-metadata-prefix invariant.

Outcome: A passing integration test that proves Phase 1 is complete — the store layer works end-to-end and is ready for the chunking engine (Phase 2) and CLI (Phase 3) to build on top of.

Do:
- Create tests/scripts/test-knowledge-integration.cjs (Node test using node:test and assert).
- This test exercises the BUILT bundle (skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs), not the source files directly. Import from the bundle to validate that esbuild bundling preserves all functionality. This catches bundling issues that unit tests on source files would miss.
- Use a temp directory for the store file and metadata (setup/teardown pattern from discovery-test-utils.cjs).
- Test scenario — insert a realistic document set:
  - At least 6 documents across multiple work units (e.g., auth-flow, data-model), multiple phases (research, discussion, specification), multiple work types (feature, epic), and multiple confidence levels (low, low-medium, medium, high).
  - Use meaningful content strings that exercise BM25 ranking (some documents should share terms, others should be distinct).
  - Generate vectors via StubProvider (128 dimensions).
- After insertion, run all three search modes and capture results:
  - Fulltext search: query a term present in multiple documents, verify ranking and correct results returned.
  - Vector search: embed a query string via StubProvider, search by vector, verify results.
  - Hybrid search: same query with both term and vector, verify combined results.
  - Filtered search: apply where clause (e.g., phase: 'specification'), verify only matching documents returned.
- NO-METADATA-PREFIX INVARIANT (design doc lines 489-493):
  - For each document inserted, assert that the stored content field is an EXACT match for the input content string — no metadata prefix prepended, no reformatting, no modification.
  - The design doc explicitly rejected prepending [phase | work_unit | topic] to content because it would pollute BM25 keyword search. This test guards that decision.
  - Example assertion: insert a document with content 'User identity uses UUID v7.' and then retrieve it (via search or direct lookup) and assert content === 'User identity uses UUID v7.' exactly, with no prefix or suffix.
- Test removal by identity:
  - Remove all chunks for one work_unit + phase + topic combination via removeByIdentity.
  - Verify removed documents no longer appear in search results.
  - Verify other documents are unaffected.
- Save the store to disk (MsgPack) and write metadata.json.
- Load the store from disk into a NEW Orama instance.
- Re-run ALL the same searches on the loaded store.
- Assert: results from the loaded store are IDENTICAL to results from the in-memory store — same documents, same order, same scores. This is the critical round-trip fidelity assertion.
- Verify metadata.json contains correct provider, model, dimensions, and last_indexed values.
- Verify document count matches before and after reload.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Test imports from the built bundle, not source files
- At least 6 documents inserted spanning multiple work units, phases, work types, and confidence levels
- Fulltext search returns correct ranked results
- Vector search returns correct ranked results
- Hybrid search returns correct ranked results
- Filtered search narrows results correctly
- Stored content field is an exact match for input content (no metadata prefix — guards design doc decision)
- removeByIdentity removes the correct documents and leaves others intact
- After save/load round-trip, all search results are identical (same docs, same order, same scores)
- Document count matches before and after round-trip
- metadata.json contains accurate provider/model/dimensions/last_indexed
- Test cleans up temp directory on completion
- All assertions pass: npm test (or node --test) runs this file successfully

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-integration.cjs
- it inserts multiple documents with vectors from StubProvider
- it returns correct fulltext search results
- it returns correct vector search results
- it returns correct hybrid search results
- it filters results by metadata enum fields
- it stores content verbatim with no metadata prefix added
- it removes documents by identity key and verifies they are gone
- it confirms non-targeted documents survive removal
- it persists store to MsgPack and reloads successfully
- it produces identical fulltext results after round-trip
- it produces identical vector results after round-trip
- it produces identical hybrid results after round-trip
- it preserves document count across round-trip
- it writes and reads metadata.json correctly

Edge Cases:
- Score comparison precision — floating point scores may differ at very low precision after serialization round-trip. Use a tolerance (e.g., 1e-6) rather than strict equality for score comparison, but strict equality for document IDs and order.
- Empty results — include at least one search that returns 0 results to verify empty-result consistency across round-trip.
- The bundle import path will be relative from the test file: something like '../../skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs'. Verify this resolves correctly.
- No-prefix invariant: test a content string that starts with a bracket character like [test] to ensure the chunker/store does not accidentally wrap or modify it.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Technical Verification Summary table (MsgPack round-trip fidelity row, esbuild bundling row), Storage section (serialization benchmarks, round-trip detail about Float32Array/toJSON), Testing Strategy section (store unit tests include remove, mock provider description), Chunk Identity and Metadata section (identity key for removal), No Metadata Prefix in Content section (lines 489-493, content stored verbatim)

title: Generic Markdown Chunking Engine + Phase Configs
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs to split workflow artifacts into semantically meaningful chunks before embedding and indexing. Arbitrary token-window splitting would cut across decisions, rationale, and context boundaries. The design doc specifies rule-based splitting on markdown headings, driven by per-phase JSON configs — the same algorithm for every phase, only the parameters change.

Solution: Implement a generic markdown chunking engine as a pure function that reads a config and splits markdown accordingly, plus all 4 phase chunking configs with settings validated against the design doc.

Outcome: A chunker module that takes (markdownString, configObject) and returns an array of chunks, plus 4 production-ready JSON configs for research, discussion, investigation, and specification.

Do:
- Create src/knowledge/chunker.js as a pure-function module. No external dependencies — just string parsing.
- The chunker function signature: chunk(markdown, config) -> array of { content } objects.
    - content: the full text of the chunk INCLUDING the heading line. Heading text serves as a semantic anchor per design doc finding #9 (line 980), ensuring the heading stays with its body for embedding and BM25 search.
    - Return shape is an array of objects (not bare strings) so future fields (e.g., line ranges, heading path) can be added without an API break. Downstream consumers (indexing pipeline, Task 3-3) destructure content from each object.
    - There is no separate heading field — the heading is embedded in the content string.

- The algorithm. Execute in the order given — the order matters and resolves ambiguity between keep_whole_below (whole-file gate) and special_sections (split-time behaviour):
  1. Strip YAML frontmatter if config.strip_frontmatter is true. Frontmatter is delimited by --- lines at the start of the file.
  2. WHOLE-FILE GATE: if the remaining content is below config.keep_whole_below lines, return a single chunk containing the whole file. Do NOT proceed to heading parsing or special_sections. This is the only way the whole-file return is produced — once a file passes this gate, it is always split.
  3. Parse markdown into sections by heading level. A section starts at a heading and includes all content until the next heading of the same or higher level. Track fenced code block state so headings inside fenced blocks do not trigger splits (standard markdown parsing correctness, not a design-doc requirement).
  4. Apply config.primary_level (typically 2 for H2) as the split level.
  5. Handle config.special_sections — a map of heading text to behaviour. Matching is exact on the trimmed heading text (case-sensitive). Values:
     - own-chunk: always split into its own chunk regardless of heading level
     - skip: exclude from the output entirely
     - merge-up: merge into the preceding section's chunk. If merge-up targets the first section (no preceding section), promote it to its own chunk.
  6. If a section exceeds config.max_lines, split it once at config.fallback_level (typically 3 for H3). If the resulting sub-sections still exceed max_lines, keep them as-is — do NOT recurse further. Design doc finding #9 (line 980) defines the fallback as a flat chain: H2 → H3 → whole file (with size warning in knowledge status). Large H3 sections are reported by status, not further split.
  7. If config.skip_empty_sections is true, skip sections with no meaningful content (only whitespace/blank lines after the heading).

- FALLBACK CHAIN FOR MISSING HEADINGS (from design doc finding #9):
    - No headings at primary_level -> try fallback_level
    - No headings at fallback_level -> return whole file as a single chunk. The knowledge status command (Phase 4) reports a size warning for whole-file chunks produced by this fallback (distinguish from whole-file returns via the keep_whole_below gate).

- H1 HANDLING:
    - Files typically have an H1 title at the top followed by content before the first H2. Treat the H1 and any content between it and the first H2 as the first chunk (H1 becomes the heading of that first chunk). This matches typical markdown document structure where the H1 is the document title and introductory content flows into the first major section.

- CRITICAL: code blocks (fenced with triple backticks) containing markdown headings must NOT trigger splits. The parser must track whether it is inside a fenced code block and skip heading detection within them. This is NOT called out in finding #9 — it is standard markdown parsing correctness. Without it, documents containing code examples with ## comments would chunk incorrectly.

- Create all 4 phase chunking configs at skills/workflow-knowledge/chunking/. Each is a JSON file following the schema from the design doc (see discussion.json example at design doc line 384). The config fields are: phase, confidence, strategy, primary_level, fallback_level, max_lines, keep_whole_below, special_sections, strip_frontmatter, skip_empty_sections.

  Config values per phase:
  - research.json: confidence low, no special sections
  - discussion.json: confidence low-medium, special_sections: Discussion Map -> own-chunk, Summary -> own-chunk
  - investigation.json: confidence medium, no special sections
  - specification.json: confidence high, no special sections
  - All share: strategy split-on-heading, primary_level 2, fallback_level 3, max_lines 200, keep_whole_below 50, strip_frontmatter true, skip_empty_sections true
  - Note: the discussion.json values are directly from the design doc example (line 384). The other three configs extrapolate the same shared defaults — these are initial values that implementers may tune if real-world testing shows per-phase adjustments are needed.

- The phase and confidence fields in each config are metadata — the chunker passes them through but does not use them. The indexing pipeline (Phase 3) reads them when constructing store documents.
- Rebuild after implementation: npm run build.

Acceptance Criteria:
- chunk(markdown, config) returns an array of { content } objects where each content includes its heading line
- H2 splitting produces one chunk per H2 section
- Sections exceeding max_lines are split once at fallback_level (H3); no further recursion
- special_sections own-chunk: Discussion Map and Summary become separate chunks
- special_sections skip: excluded sections do not appear in output
- special_sections merge-up: section content is appended to the preceding chunk; first-section merge-up promotes to its own chunk
- YAML frontmatter is stripped when configured
- Empty sections are skipped when configured
- Files below keep_whole_below return as a single chunk BEFORE any heading parsing or special_section handling
- No headings at primary level falls back to fallback level
- No headings at any level returns whole file as single chunk
- H1 + pre-H2 content forms the first chunk with H1 as its heading
- Code blocks containing headings do not trigger false splits
- All 4 JSON configs exist at skills/workflow-knowledge/chunking/ and match the documented settings
- npm run build succeeds with the chunker module included

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-chunker.cjs (Node test using node:test and assert. No external deps — pure function testing.)
- it splits markdown on H2 headings
- it returns { content } objects, not bare strings
- it includes heading text in each chunks content (semantic anchor, per finding #9)
- it splits oversized H2 sections once at H3 and does not recurse further
- it keeps oversized H3 sections as-is (flat fallback chain)
- it falls back to H3 when no H2 headings exist
- it returns whole file when no headings at any configured level
- it returns whole file when content is below keep_whole_below, bypassing heading parsing
- it strips YAML frontmatter
- it skips empty sections
- it handles own-chunk special sections
- it handles skip special sections
- it handles merge-up special sections
- it handles merge-up on the first section (promotes to its own chunk)
- it treats H1 + pre-H2 content as the first chunk
- it ignores headings inside fenced code blocks (markdown parsing correctness)
- it handles file with only frontmatter and no content (empty result)

Edge Cases:
- Markdown with no headings at all — returns single chunk (whole file) via the missing-headings fallback, not the keep_whole_below gate
- Code block containing ## — must not split. Track fenced block state (backtick-fence opens/closes). This is markdown parsing correctness, not a design-doc requirement.
- Nested code blocks — rare but should not break the parser
- Frontmatter with --- appearing later in the file (e.g., horizontal rules) — only strip the opening frontmatter block, not content after it
- Section with heading but no body text — treated as empty, skipped if configured
- Very large file (1000+ lines) — should still chunk correctly, just produces more chunks
- File just above keep_whole_below with very few headings — heading parsing runs; if no primary/fallback headings exist, returns whole file via the missing-headings fallback. Two whole-file paths (gate vs fallback) exist for different reasons.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Phase-Aware Chunking section (algorithm steps, chunk size table, config schema example at line 384, special_sections values), Chunking edge cases finding #9 (line 980, fallback chain is flat H2 -> H3 -> whole file, heading as semantic anchor). Note: fenced code block handling is markdown parsing correctness and not part of finding #9 — it is a necessary implementation detail for the 'split on markdown headings' strategy to work correctly.

title: Chunker Validation with Real Fixtures
description:
Problem: Task 2-1 builds and tests the chunking engine with synthetic markdown. But real workflow artifacts have structural patterns that synthetic tests may not capture — discussions have Discussion Maps with specific formatting, research files can be 500-1300 lines with varied section structures, specifications have nested subsections, and investigations follow a specific Symptoms/Analysis/Fix Direction template. The production configs need to be validated against realistic data to confirm chunk boundaries land in the right places.

Solution: Create representative test fixtures for all 4 indexed phases and validate that the production configs produce correct, well-bounded chunks.

Outcome: A comprehensive fixture-based test suite that proves the chunking engine handles real-world artifact structures correctly, giving confidence that the production configs are tuned properly.

Do:
- Create test fixtures in tests/fixtures/knowledge/ (one file per phase, plus edge case files):
  - research-fixture.md: a realistic research artifact with H2 sections (findings, evidence, recommendations), sized within the design doc's real-world range of 500-1300 lines (line 368), with some sections large enough to trigger fallback splitting
  - discussion-fixture.md: a realistic discussion with H1 title, Discussion Map section, 4-6 H2 subtopics each with Context/Options/Journey/Decision subsections, and a Summary section. 150-300 lines.
  - investigation-fixture.md: a realistic investigation with Symptoms, Analysis, and Fix Direction H2 sections, each with H3 subsections. ~150 lines.
  - specification-fixture.md: a realistic specification with H2 sections for requirements, constraints, edge cases, dependencies. 150-400 lines.
- Fixtures should mimic the structure of real workflow artifacts. Reference the design doc chunk size table (line 366) for typical sizes:
  - Research: 50-200 lines per chunk, 5-15 chunks per file (500-1300 lines total)
  - Discussion: 20-80 lines per subtopic, 6-9 subtopics typical
  - Investigation: 30-60 lines per section, ~150 lines total
  - Specification: 50-150 lines per section
- Also create edge case fixtures:
  - discussion-no-map-fixture.md: discussion without a Discussion Map section
  - research-single-section-fixture.md: research with only one H2 and 600+ lines (must trigger fallback to H3)
  - spec-minimal-fixture.md: specification below keep_whole_below threshold (should be single chunk)
  - artifact-with-codeblocks-fixture.md: content containing fenced code blocks with ## headings inside them
  - artifact-no-headings-fixture.md: content with no markdown headings at all
- For each fixture, test with the corresponding production config (loaded from skills/workflow-knowledge/chunking/) and assert:
  - Correct number of chunks
  - Each chunk starts with its expected heading
  - No chunk exceeds max_lines
  - Special sections (Discussion Map, Summary) are correctly isolated as own-chunk
  - Content is verbatim — no text is modified during chunking (character-for-character match against the source)

CONTENT PRESERVATION (verbatim guarantee):
- The knowledge base design is explicit: no information loss (design doc line 74). Each chunk must be verbatim text from the source — no modifications, no added metadata prefix, no reformatting.
- Test this at the chunk level: for every chunk, assert that its content appears verbatim as a substring of the original file (after frontmatter stripping).
- DO NOT test content preservation via concatenation. Concatenating all chunks will NOT reproduce the original file because:
  - Sections with special_sections: skip are intentionally omitted
  - Empty sections (skip_empty_sections: true) are dropped
  - Frontmatter is stripped
- The correct invariant is: each chunks content is a verbatim substring of the source, AND the union of chunk contents covers all non-skipped, non-empty sections.

Acceptance Criteria:
- At least 4 realistic fixtures exist (one per indexed phase)
- At least 4 edge case fixtures exist
- Each realistic fixture produces the expected number of chunks with correct boundaries
- Discussion fixture: Discussion Map and Summary are separate own-chunks
- Research fixture with oversized section: fallback splitting on H3 produces sub-chunks
- Minimal spec fixture: returns as single chunk (below keep_whole_below)
- Code block fixture: headings inside code blocks do not trigger splits
- No-headings fixture: returns as single chunk
- Content preservation: each chunks content is a verbatim substring of the source file
- All tests pass

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-chunker.cjs (extend the file from Task 2-1 with a new describe block for fixture-based tests)
- it chunks a research fixture into expected sections
- it chunks a discussion fixture with Discussion Map and Summary as own-chunks
- it chunks an investigation fixture into Symptoms, Analysis, Fix Direction sections
- it chunks a specification fixture into expected sections
- it handles discussion without Discussion Map gracefully
- it triggers fallback splitting on oversized research section
- it returns minimal spec as single chunk
- it ensures each chunks content is a verbatim substring of the source
- it handles code blocks with headings without false splits
- it returns no-headings fixture as single chunk

Edge Cases:
- Fixture with trailing whitespace or blank lines at end of file — should not produce empty trailing chunk
- Fixture with Windows line endings (CRLF) — chunker should handle both LF and CRLF
- Discussion Map with varying formats — some may use checkboxes, others plain lists. The heading match should be on section name, not content format.
- Research with H2 and H3 mixed — primary split on H2, only fallback to H3 within oversized H2 sections (not globally)
- Fixtures with sections marked skip via special_sections config — those sections must NOT appear in any chunks content

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Phase-Aware Chunking section (chunk size table at line 366 showing real-world sizes per phase, config example at line 384), Testing Strategy section (chunking unit tests with fixtures from real artifacts), No information loss principle (line 74)

title: Config Resolution + Provider Instantiation + CLI Entry Point
description:
Problem: The knowledge CLI needs foundational wiring before any command can function: configuration reading from two levels, provider instantiation based on that config, and a command dispatch entry point. Without this, no command knows which provider to use, where the store lives, or what settings to apply.

Solution: Implement config resolution (system + project merge), provider instantiation from config (three modes), and CLI command dispatch following the manifest.cjs pattern.

Outcome: A working CLI skeleton that reads config, instantiates the correct provider, dispatches commands, parses flags, and reports errors — the foundation every Phase 3 command plugs into.

TERMINOLOGY DISAMBIGUATION (critical — the design doc uses two similar terms):
- StubProvider (Task 1-2) — a TEST provider that returns fake vectors. Instantiated when config.provider is stub. ALWAYS returns vectors. Used for development and testing.
- Stub mode / keyword-only mode (design doc lines 322-331) — a PRODUCTION degraded mode triggered when NO provider is configured OR no API key is available. Documents stored WITHOUT vectors, fulltext search only.
These are DIFFERENT. provider: stub instantiates StubProvider (still returns vectors). Absence of provider entirely (provider field missing, or api_key_env resolves to empty) triggers keyword-only mode (no provider instance, no vectors). The resolveProvider function below MUST handle both correctly.

Do:

CONFIG RESOLUTION (new src/knowledge/config.js):
- System config location: ~/.config/workflows/config.json
- Project config location: .workflows/.knowledge/config.json
- Both are optional — the CLI must handle either or both being absent.
- CONFIG FILE STRUCTURE: both config files wrap all knowledge-base settings under a top-level `"knowledge"` key (matching the design doc examples at lines 262-301). Example:
    { "knowledge": { "provider": "openai", "model": "text-embedding-3-small", ... } }
  Reads must unwrap this key — `loadConfig()` returns the merged contents of the inner `knowledge` object, not the outer wrapper. Writes (e.g., by the setup wizard in Task 7-1) must include the wrapper.
- Config schema (fields inside the `knowledge` object, from design doc lines 289-301):
    provider: string (e.g. stub, openai)
    model: string (e.g. text-embedding-3-small)
    dimensions: number (e.g. 1536)
    api_key_env: string (e.g. OPENAI_API_KEY) — name of env var, NOT the key itself
    similarity_threshold: number (default 0.8)
    decay_months: number (default 6)
- Resolution: project config inherits from system config and overrides what it specifies. Missing fields fall through to the system level. If a field is absent from both, use defaults.
- API key resolution: read the env var named by api_key_env from process.env. If the env var is not set, the key is absent (triggers keyword-only mode, not an error).
- Export a loadConfig() function that returns the merged (and unwrapped) config object.

PROVIDER INSTANTIATION (extend src/knowledge/config.js or new resolver):
- Three modes based on config:
    1. provider is stub -> instantiate StubProvider from Phase 1 (Task 1-2). This is a TEST path. Dimensions from config or StubProvider default (128). Used for development and testing. The store will contain vectors (fake ones).
    2. provider is openai (or other future provider) -> check if the provider implementation exists. In the plan's Phase 3, only stub is available; OpenAI comes in Phase 4. If the provider is not yet implemented, exit with a clear error: Provider [name] is not available. Available providers: stub
    3. No provider configured (provider field missing from merged config) OR api_key_env is set but the env var resolves to empty -> KEYWORD-ONLY MODE (stub mode in the design doc's terminology). Return null. The CLI still functions — index stores documents WITHOUT vectors, query uses fulltext only. This is NOT an error — it is a supported degraded mode per design doc lines 322-331.
- Export a resolveProvider(config) function that returns a provider instance or null (null = keyword-only mode).
- CRITICAL: do not conflate provider: stub (test provider, returns vectors) with keyword-only mode (no provider, no vectors). They produce different store contents: stub-mode stores contain vectors from StubProvider; keyword-only-mode stores have no vectors at all.

CLI ENTRY POINT (extend src/knowledge/index.js):
- Replace the minimal validation code from Task 1-1 with a real CLI entry point.
- Parse process.argv: first positional arg is the command, remaining args are command-specific.
- Command dispatch (same switch pattern as manifest.cjs lines 914-933):
    index, query, check, status, remove, compact, rebuild, setup
  Phase 3 implements: index, query, check. Others exit with: Command [name] is not yet implemented.
- Flag parsing for common flags across commands:
    --work-type, --phase, --work-unit, --topic, --limit, --dry-run
  Parse these into an options object passed to command handlers.
- No command or unknown command: print usage summary and exit with code 1.
- Each command handler receives (args, options, config, provider) — all the context needed to operate.
- Rebuild after implementation: npm run build.

Acceptance Criteria:
- loadConfig() reads both config files with the top-level `knowledge` wrapper and returns the unwrapped merged object
- loadConfig() merges system and project configs correctly (project overrides system)
- loadConfig() returns defaults when both configs are absent
- loadConfig() resolves api_key_env from process.env
- resolveProvider returns a StubProvider instance when config.provider is stub
- resolveProvider returns null when no provider field is configured (keyword-only mode)
- resolveProvider returns null when api_key_env is set but the env var is empty (keyword-only mode)
- resolveProvider errors clearly for unimplemented providers (e.g., openai before Phase 4)
- CLI dispatches known commands to the correct handler
- CLI prints usage and exits 1 for unknown/missing commands
- Flag parsing extracts --work-type, --phase, --work-unit, --limit correctly
- Missing required flags produce clear error messages
- npm run build succeeds

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-config.cjs (Node test for config resolution and provider instantiation)
- it reads a config file with the `knowledge` wrapper and returns the unwrapped inner object
- it handles a config file missing the `knowledge` wrapper as an invalid-format error (or empty, implementer's call — but must not silently return wrong shape)
- it merges system and project configs with project taking precedence
- it returns defaults when no config files exist
- it resolves api_key_env from environment
- it returns null api key when env var is not set
- it instantiates StubProvider for provider stub (test path returns a provider instance)
- it returns null provider when no provider field configured (keyword-only mode)
- it returns null provider when env var resolves to empty (keyword-only mode)
- it errors for unknown provider names
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (shell test for CLI dispatch — tests against the built bundle)
- it prints usage for no command
- it prints usage for unknown command
- it dispatches to check without error
- it reports not-yet-implemented for unbuilt commands

Edge Cases:
- System config exists but project config does not — system values used for all fields
- Project config exists but system config does not — project values used, missing fields get defaults
- Neither config exists — all defaults applied. No provider field set, so keyword-only mode.
- Config file is missing the `knowledge` wrapper (e.g., fields at top level) — clear error about expected format
- api_key_env field is set but the env var itself is empty string — treat as absent (keyword-only mode)
- Config file exists but contains invalid JSON — clear error with file path
- Config file has extra unknown fields inside `knowledge` — ignore them, do not error (forward compatibility)
- Config has provider: stub but no api_key_env — StubProvider doesn't need an API key, this is valid (test path)

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Configuration Hierarchy section (two-level config, resolution rules, complete schema at lines 262-301 all showing the `knowledge` wrapper), Knowledge Base is Required Infrastructure section (stub mode / keyword-only description at lines 322-331), Embedding Provider section (StubProvider at lines 229-257 and 469), and the note that API keys are always referenced by env var name, never stored directly

title: Manifest Resolve Command
description:
Problem: The knowledge CLI needs to map work_unit + phase + topic to file paths on disk — for bulk discovery in Phase 4 and for validating identity derivation in Phase 3. The manifest CLI already encodes all directory conventions internally, but does not expose them as resolved paths. Duplicating path conventions across scripts would create drift risk.

Solution: Add a resolve command to the existing manifest CLI (skills/workflow-manifest/scripts/manifest.cjs) that returns file paths for a given work_unit.phase.topic.

Outcome: A single authoritative command for resolving workflow artifact paths, usable by the knowledge CLI and any future tooling that needs to locate files.

Do:
- Add a resolve command to skills/workflow-manifest/scripts/manifest.cjs following the existing command pattern (new case in the switch statement at line 920, new cmdResolve function).
- Invocation: manifest.cjs resolve <work_unit>.<phase>.<topic>
- The command uses the existing parsePath function (which splits on dots into segments) to extract work_unit, phase, and topic.
- For each indexed phase, return the file path(s) where the artifact lives:
    - discussion: .workflows/{work_unit}/discussion/{topic}.md (single file)
    - investigation: .workflows/{work_unit}/investigation/{topic}.md (single file)
    - specification: .workflows/{work_unit}/specification/{topic}/specification.md (single file)
    - research: .workflows/{work_unit}/research/ — research is special. Research in epics can have multiple files with non-predictable names (exploration.md, networking.md, etc.). The manifest's phases.research.items is the authoritative source — iterate the items to produce paths. Do NOT scan the directory; drift between manifest state and filesystem is a separate concern handled by the knowledge status command. If topic is provided (3 segments), return the specific file path for that item. If only work_unit.research is given (2 segments), iterate phases.research.items and return one path per item.
- Output: one file path per line on stdout. Single-file phases output one line. Research may output multiple lines.
- Validate that the work unit exists (read its manifest). Validate that the phase is valid. If the resolved path does not exist on disk, still output it but exit with code 0 — the path is correct even if the file has not been created yet. This matches how other manifest commands work (they report state, not filesystem).
- Error cases: unknown work unit (exit 1 with error), invalid phase (exit 1 with error), insufficient segments (exit 1 with usage hint).
- Only indexed phases need to be supported: research, discussion, investigation, specification. If a non-indexed phase is requested (planning, implementation, review), exit with error: Phase [name] is not indexed by the knowledge base.
- Update the usage string in the die() call at line 917 to include resolve in the command list.
- Add resolve to the CLAUDE.md documentation if the manifest CLI commands are listed there (check first).

Acceptance Criteria:
- manifest.cjs resolve auth-flow.discussion.auth-flow outputs .workflows/auth-flow/discussion/auth-flow.md
- manifest.cjs resolve auth-flow.specification.auth-flow outputs .workflows/auth-flow/specification/auth-flow/specification.md
- manifest.cjs resolve auth-flow.investigation.auth-flow outputs .workflows/auth-flow/investigation/auth-flow.md
- manifest.cjs resolve payments.research.exploration outputs the path for that specific research item (from phases.research.items)
- manifest.cjs resolve payments.research (2 segments) iterates phases.research.items and outputs one path per item
- Resolve does NOT scan the research directory — it reads from the manifest's phases.research.items only
- Non-existent work unit exits 1 with clear error
- Non-indexed phase exits 1 with clear error
- Missing segments exits 1 with usage hint
- Resolved paths are correct even when the file does not yet exist on disk

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-workflow-manifest.sh (extend the existing manifest test file with new test functions for resolve)
- it resolves a discussion file path
- it resolves a specification file path (nested topic directory)
- it resolves an investigation file path
- it resolves a single research file path (3-segment form)
- it resolves all research files for a work unit (2-segment form, from phases.research.items)
- it does NOT include research files that exist on disk but are not in phases.research.items (manifest is authoritative)
- it errors for non-existent work unit
- it errors for non-indexed phase (e.g., planning)
- it errors for insufficient segments (just work unit name)
- it outputs correct path even when file does not exist on disk

Edge Cases:
- Research with non-predictable filenames — resolve reads from phases.research.items, which tracks whatever item names were used when the research was authored. No naming convention assumptions.
- Research items listed in manifest but file deleted from disk — resolve still returns the path (manifest is the source of truth). The knowledge status command handles the filesystem drift separately (orphan detection).
- Research files on disk but not in phases.research.items — resolve does NOT return these (they were never tracked). This is correct behaviour; ad-hoc research files are not indexed.
- Work unit with dots in the name — the parsePath function splits on dots, so work unit names with dots would break parsing. Verify this cannot happen (manifest init likely rejects dots in names).
- Specification path has an extra nesting level ({topic}/specification.md) compared to discussion/investigation ({topic}.md) — the resolve function must handle this per-phase path convention difference.
- Epic vs feature research — epics have multiple research topics in phases.research.items, features typically have one. The 2-segment form must handle both.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — the manifest resolve command description (lines 479-487), specifically line 487: 'The manifest's phases.research.items tracks individual research topics — resolve returns the path for each.' The manifest is authoritative for research items; directory scanning is explicitly not used.

title: Index File Command
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs to accept a single artifact file, chunk it, embed it, and store the chunks — this is the primary indexing pathway used by processing skills at phase completion. This command wires together every module built so far: config, provider, chunker, store, and persistence.

Solution: Implement the knowledge index <file> command that performs end-to-end single-file indexing: derive identity from the file path, load the appropriate chunking config, chunk the file, embed chunks via the provider, remove any existing chunks for the same identity key, insert new chunks, and save the store.

Outcome: A working index command that can take any completed workflow artifact file and add it to the knowledge base, with re-indexing replacing previous chunks and provider mismatch detection preventing silent corruption.

Do:
- Implement the index command handler in the CLI (invoked by Task 3-1 dispatch).
- Invocation: knowledge index <source_file>
- The source_file is an absolute or relative path to a workflow artifact (e.g., .workflows/auth-flow/discussion/auth-flow.md).

IDENTITY DERIVATION:
- Parse the file path to extract work_unit, phase, and topic. The path follows known conventions:
    .workflows/{work_unit}/discussion/{topic}.md
    .workflows/{work_unit}/investigation/{topic}.md
    .workflows/{work_unit}/specification/{topic}/specification.md
    .workflows/{work_unit}/research/{filename}.md
- For research files, the topic is the filename without extension (e.g., exploration.md -> topic is exploration).
- Validate the derived phase is an indexed phase (research, discussion, investigation, specification). If not, exit with error: File is in phase [name] which is not indexed.
- Also derive work_type by reading the work unit manifest (manifest.json in the work unit directory) to get the work_type field. This is needed as metadata on each chunk.

CHUNKING:
- Load the phase chunking config from skills/workflow-knowledge/chunking/{phase}.json. Use path.join(__dirname, '..', 'chunking', phase + '.json') to find it — this is the runtime config discovery path that Task 1-1 validated.
- Read the source file content.
- Run the chunker from Phase 2 (Task 2-1): chunk(content, config) -> array of chunks, where each chunk has a content field containing the full text including the heading line.

PROVIDER / STORE STATE RESOLUTION (before embedding):
- Load the existing store from disk (via loadStore from Task 1-4). If the store file does not exist, create a new empty store (via createStore from Task 1-3). Also check if metadata.json exists alongside the store.
- metadata.json initialisation (first-index case): if metadata.json does not exist yet, create it with the full schema from Task 1-4: { provider, model, dimensions, last_indexed, pending: [] }. The provider/model/dimensions come from the current resolved config — or all three null if the current config has no provider (keyword-only mode). The last_indexed is set to the current timestamp. The pending array MUST be initialised as an empty array — subsequent tasks (Task 4-4) write to it. Never write metadata.json without the pending field, even on first creation.
- Provider state branching (covers four cases — this logic must match Task 3-4 query's provider state handling so indexing and querying never disagree about the store's mode):
    1. metadata has provider AND current config has provider AND they match (same provider/model/dimensions): proceed with full embedding via the provider. Normal path.
    2. metadata has provider AND current config has a DIFFERENT provider (or dimensions/model differ): refuse with "Provider/model changed since last index. Run knowledge rebuild to reindex." Exit non-zero. This is the hard mismatch case — the existing vectors are incompatible with the new provider.
    3. metadata has provider AND current config has NO provider (user removed their API key): refuse with the same mismatch error. Degrading a full-mode store to keyword-only requires an explicit rebuild so the user sees what they are giving up.
    4. metadata.provider is null (store was built keyword-only) — this is always allowed regardless of whether the current config has a provider or not. Index WITHOUT vectors (omit the embedding field, do not call provider.embed). This preserves the store's keyword-only state. The user sees the stub-to-full upgrade note on query output (Task 3-4) and in knowledge status (Task 4-6), and can run knowledge rebuild when they choose. This is the symmetric counterpart to Task 3-4 query's stub-to-full path — both commands tolerate the upgrade window without forcing an immediate rebuild, so phase completions do not pile up in the pending queue.
- Implementer rationale: without case (4), a project that starts keyword-only and later adds an API key would have every subsequent phase completion refuse to index, filling the pending queue with permanently-failing items. Design doc finding #13 (line 992) and the upgrade-at-your-own-pace intent depend on this tolerance.

EMBEDDING:
- If the resolved state (above) is case 1 (full embedding path): call provider.embedBatch() with all chunk content strings. This produces one vector per chunk in a single call.
- If the resolved state is case 4 (keyword-only store): do not call the provider at all. Chunks will be stored without the embedding field (not null — Orama crashes on null per Task 1-2).

STORE OPERATIONS:
- Remove existing chunks for the identity key: removeByIdentity(db, { work_unit, phase, topic }) from Task 1-3. This makes re-indexing idempotent.
- Construct chunk documents with all schema fields:
    id: {work_unit}-{phase}-{topic}-{seq} (seq is zero-padded 3-digit: 001, 002, ...)
    content: chunk.content (verbatim — no metadata prefix per design doc)
    work_unit, work_type, phase, topic: from identity derivation
    confidence: from the chunking config (config.confidence)
    source_file: the original file path argument
    timestamp: Date.now()
    embedding: vector from provider, or omitted for keyword-only stores (cases 4)
- Insert all chunk documents via insertDocument from Task 1-3.

PERSISTENCE:
- Acquire file lock (withLock from Task 1-4).
- Save the store to disk (saveStore from Task 1-4).
- Update metadata.json: set last_indexed to current timestamp. Do NOT touch the pending array here (that is owned by Task 4-4's retry mechanism). Do NOT overwrite provider/model/dimensions if they already match the current config — and in case 4 (keyword-only store) explicitly do NOT upgrade them to match the new config. Rebuild is the only path that changes stored provider fields.
- Release lock.
- Output: brief summary to stdout — Indexed {N} chunks from {source_file}

Acceptance Criteria:
- knowledge index .workflows/auth-flow/discussion/auth-flow.md indexes the file and outputs chunk count
- Chunks have correct identity fields (work_unit, phase, topic derived from path)
- Chunks have correct work_type (read from work unit manifest)
- Chunks have confidence from the phase chunking config
- Chunk IDs follow the {work_unit}-{phase}-{topic}-{seq} format
- Content is stored verbatim (no metadata prefix)
- Re-indexing the same file replaces previous chunks (not duplicates)
- Full-provider mismatch (case 2) is detected and refused with clear message
- Config-has-no-provider against a full store (case 3) is refused with the same message
- Keyword-only store with provider in config (case 4) indexes WITHOUT vectors, does not refuse, does not upgrade stored provider fields
- Store is saved to disk after indexing
- metadata.json is created on first index with full schema including empty pending array
- metadata.json last_indexed is updated after each successful index
- metadata.json pending array is untouched by the index command (owned by Task 4-4)
- metadata.json provider/model/dimensions are never upgraded by index (only by rebuild)
- File lock is acquired during write and released after

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (extend the CLI shell test file from Task 3-1)
- it indexes a discussion file and reports chunk count
- it indexes a specification file (nested path convention)
- it indexes a research file
- it re-indexes a file and replaces previous chunks (chunk count stays same, not doubled)
- it refuses indexing on full-provider mismatch
- it refuses indexing when config has no provider but store has vectors
- it indexes WITHOUT vectors when store is keyword-only and config has a provider (upgrade window tolerance)
- it leaves metadata.json provider/model/dimensions unchanged after case-4 indexing
- it indexes in keyword-only mode from scratch (no provider configured, no existing store)
- it errors for a file not in .workflows/ path structure
- it errors for a file in a non-indexed phase
- it creates metadata.json with empty pending array on first index
- it updates last_indexed without touching the pending array on subsequent indexes

Edge Cases:
- File does not exist — clear error with the path
- File is empty — chunker returns empty array, zero chunks indexed (not an error)
- File in .workflows/ but not matching expected path pattern — clear error explaining expected structure
- Store file does not exist yet (first index) — create new store AND new metadata.json with full schema
- metadata.json exists but is missing the pending field (e.g., manually edited) — treat missing pending as empty array for robustness
- Very large file producing many chunks — embedBatch handles the full array in one call
- Research file with unusual filename — topic derived from filename stem, no assumptions about naming

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — knowledge index command (lines 639-642), Chunk Identity and Metadata section (identity key, chunk ID format, fields), No Metadata Prefix in Content section, Indexing section (phase completion trigger, indexed phases), Provider mismatch detection finding #10 (line 982), Stub-to-full upgrade detection finding #13 (line 992, upgrade window is tolerated until the user runs rebuild), Error Handling section (lines 780-788, pending queue schema)

title: Query Command + Output Formatting + Re-ranking
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs to be searchable. The query command is the primary retrieval interface — used autonomously by Claude during workflow phases and explicitly at planning entry for cross-cutting context. It must support hybrid search (text + vector), metadata filtering, post-processing re-ranking, and formatted output with provenance.

Solution: Implement the knowledge query command with hybrid/fulltext search, flag-based filtering, application-level re-ranking, and the output format specified in the design doc.

Outcome: A query command that accepts natural language search terms, searches the knowledge base with filtering and re-ranking, and returns formatted results with provenance metadata — ready for Claude to consume during workflow phases.

Do:
- Implement the query command handler in the CLI (invoked by Task 3-1 dispatch).
- Invocation: knowledge query <search_term> [--work-type <type,...>] [--phase <phase,...>] [--work-unit <wu>] [--limit N]

PROVIDER MISMATCH DETECTION (before embedding):
- Query DOES embed the search term via provider.embed before running hybrid search, so a config-vs-store provider mismatch matters. Detection is symmetric with Task 3-3 index.
- Read metadata.json (via readMetadata from Task 1-4). Compare metadata.provider / metadata.model / metadata.dimensions against the current merged config (from Task 3-1).
- If dimensions differ: refuse with the same error as index: "Provider/model changed (dimensions mismatch). Run `knowledge rebuild` to reindex." Exit non-zero. Running a query with mismatched dimensions would either crash Orama or produce garbage results — neither is acceptable.
- If dimensions match but provider or model differs: the vector space is effectively different; semantic results would be garbage. Refuse with the same message. User rebuilds before querying.
