- If metadata.provider is null (store built keyword-only) but current config has a provider: this is the stub-to-full upgrade case. Do NOT refuse — fall through to the keyword-only path and prepend the upgrade note (see below).
- If all match: proceed with the normal search flow.
- This check matches index-time behavior and keeps the store-vs-config invariant enforced on every command that touches vectors.

SEARCH:
- If provider is available (not keyword-only mode) and store has vectors:
    1. Embed the search term via provider.embed(search_term) to get a query vector.
    2. Run searchHybrid from Task 1-4 with both the term and vector.
    3. Pass similarity (from config similarity_threshold, default 0.8 per design doc finding #15) to the search wrapper.
    4. Default hybridWeights: text 0.4, vector 0.6 (from design doc).
- If provider is null (keyword-only mode, matches store):
    1. Run searchFulltext from Task 1-3 with just the term.
    2. No vector search possible.
    3. Prepend a note to output: [keyword-only mode — configure embedding provider for semantic search]
- If store is keyword-only but current config has a provider (stub-to-full upgrade):
    1. Run searchFulltext from Task 1-3 — cannot use vectors, the store has none.
    2. Prepend the upgrade note: [keyword-only mode store — run `knowledge rebuild` for semantic search with your configured provider]
    3. Design doc finding #13 (line 992): this upgrade opportunity surfaces naturally on query output, not just status.

FILTERING:
- --work-type and --phase map to Orama where clause. Both accept comma-separated values mapped to in-list filters (e.g., --phase specification,discussion -> { phase: { in: ['specification', 'discussion'] } }).
- --work-unit is NOT a filter — it is a re-ranking hint. Results from the specified work unit are boosted in post-processing. Cross-work-unit results still appear, just ranked lower.
- --limit caps the number of results returned (default 10).
- Load the store from disk (loadStore from Task 1-4) before searching. Read-only — no lock needed per design doc.

POST-PROCESSING RE-RANKING (from design doc lines 566-574):
- After getting results from Orama, apply application-level score adjustments:
    1. Work-unit proximity: if --work-unit is provided, boost scores of results matching that work unit. This is a hint, not a filter — results from other work units still appear.
    2. Confidence tier: higher confidence results get a small boost when relevance scores are similar. Spec (high) > investigation (medium) > discussion (low-medium) > research (low).
    3. Recency: more recent chunks (higher timestamp) get a small boost.
- Re-sort by adjusted score after applying all boosts.
- This is ~15 lines of code on a small result set (limit 10) per design doc. Keep it simple.

OUTPUT FORMAT (from design doc lines 580-603):
- Plain text, not JSON — Claude reads and incorporates, does not parse programmatically.
- Format:
    [N results]

    [phase | work_unit/topic | confidence | YYYY-MM-DD]
    chunk content verbatim
    Source: source_file_path

    [phase | work_unit/topic | confidence | YYYY-MM-DD]
    chunk content verbatim
    Source: source_file_path
- Result count header.
- Each chunk: provenance line in square brackets, content verbatim, source file path.
- Date is the chunk timestamp formatted as YYYY-MM-DD.
- Blank line between chunks.
- Empty results: [0 results]
- Stub mode note (when applicable): [keyword-only mode — configure embedding provider for semantic search] as the first line, before the result count.
- Stub-to-full upgrade note (when store is keyword-only but config has a provider): [keyword-only mode store — run `knowledge rebuild` for semantic search with your configured provider] as the first line.

Acceptance Criteria:
- knowledge query <term> returns formatted results with provenance
- Hybrid search works when provider is available and store has vectors (both text and vector scoring)
- Fulltext-only search works in keyword-only mode
- Provider mismatch detected at query time and refused with the same error as index (symmetric with Task 3-3)
- Stub-to-full upgrade note appears when store is keyword-only but config has a provider
- --work-type filters results to matching work types
- --phase filters results to matching phases
- --work-unit boosts matching results without excluding others
- --limit caps result count
- similarity_threshold is read from config and passed to the search wrapper
- Re-ranking visibly affects result order (test with work-unit proximity)
- Output format matches design doc exactly (provenance line, content, source)
- Empty results output [0 results]

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (extend CLI shell tests)
- Setup: index several fixture files first (using the index command from Task 3-3), then query against them.
- it returns formatted results for a matching query
- it returns [0 results] for a non-matching query
- it filters by --phase
- it filters by --work-type
- it respects --limit
- it boosts --work-unit results (same query with and without --work-unit produces different ordering)
- it works in keyword-only mode with stub note
- it refuses query on provider mismatch (same error as index mismatch)
- it shows stub-to-full upgrade note when store is keyword-only but config has a provider
- it formats output with provenance line, content, and source path

Edge Cases:
- Query with no flags — searches all, default limit 10
- Query in empty store — returns [0 results], no error
- --work-type with comma-separated values — both types included in filter
- Very long search term — should still embed and search without error
- Results with identical scores — stable sort (preserve Orama ordering)
- Re-ranking when all results are from the same work unit — proximity boost has no effect (all boosted equally)
- Provider mismatch with null fields (missing from metadata) — treat as mismatch, refuse (defensive — the metadata should always have all fields after Task 1-4, but a partially-written file could slip through)

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — knowledge query command (lines 658-664), Retrieved Context Format section (lines 580-603, exact output format), Retrieval Weighting section (lines 566-574, re-ranking factors), Provider mismatch detection finding #10 (line 982, query now embeds and must also detect mismatch), similarity_threshold configurability finding #15 (line 999), Stub-to-full upgrade detection finding #13 (line 992, upgrade note surfaces on query output)

title: Check Command + CLI Shell Tests
description:
Problem: Entry-point skills need a fast, reliable way to check if the knowledge base is set up before proceeding. The check command is the gate — it runs in Step 0 of every skill invocation. It must never produce false errors in Claude Code (always exit 0), and must be unambiguous (stdout is ready or not-ready). Additionally, all Phase 3 commands need shell tests validating stdout, stderr, and exit codes against the built bundle.

Solution: Implement the knowledge check command and a comprehensive shell test suite covering all Phase 3 CLI commands (check, index, query).

Outcome: A check command that reliably reports readiness, plus a test suite that validates the entire Phase 3 CLI works correctly as a built bundle.

Do:

CHECK COMMAND:
- Implement the check command handler in the CLI (invoked by Task 3-1 dispatch).
- Invocation: knowledge check (no arguments, no flags)
- Three conditions must ALL pass for ready:
    1. .workflows/.knowledge/ directory exists
    2. .workflows/.knowledge/config.json exists
    3. .workflows/.knowledge/store.msp exists and is loadable (not corrupted — attempt MsgPack decode, catch errors)
- If all three pass: output ready to stdout, exit 0.
- If any fail: output not-ready to stdout, exit 0.
- CRITICAL: exit code is ALWAYS 0 unless there is a genuine system error (e.g., unreadable filesystem). Skills branch on the stdout value, not the exit code. Exit 1 would produce errors in Claude Code and break the Step 0 flow.
- No other output — no explanatory text, no suggestions. Just ready or not-ready. The hard stop reference file in Phase 5 handles the user-facing explanation.

CLI SHELL TEST SUITE:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (consolidate all Phase 3 CLI tests into one shell test file)
- Follow existing shell test conventions: PASS/FAIL counters, assert_eq function, setup/teardown with temp dirs, summary line.
- Tests run against the BUILT bundle (node skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs), not source files.
- Setup: create a temp directory simulating a project with .workflows/ structure. Create fixture artifact files. Write config files (both system and project level). Use provider: stub in config so all tests work without an API key.
- Test categories:

  Check command tests:
  - it outputs ready when directory + config + store all exist
  - it outputs not-ready when directory is missing
  - it outputs not-ready when config is missing
  - it outputs not-ready when store is missing
  - it outputs not-ready when store is corrupted (write garbage bytes)
  - it always exits 0 in all above cases

  Index command tests (from Task 3-3):
  - it indexes a fixture file and reports chunk count
  - it re-indexes the same file without duplicating chunks
  - it refuses indexing on provider mismatch (write metadata with different provider, then index)
  - it errors for a non-existent file
  - it errors for a non-indexed phase file

  Query command tests (from Task 3-4):
  - it returns formatted results for a matching query
  - it returns [0 results] for a non-matching query
  - it filters by --phase flag
  - it respects --limit flag
  - it outputs stub mode note in keyword-only mode
  - it formats output with provenance line, content, and source path

  CLI dispatch tests (from Task 3-1):
  - it prints usage for no command
  - it prints usage for unknown command
  - it reports not-yet-implemented for Phase 4 commands

Acceptance Criteria:
- knowledge check outputs exactly ready or not-ready on stdout
- Exit code is 0 in all check scenarios (ready, not-ready, corrupted store)
- All check conditions tested: full setup, missing dir, missing config, missing store, corrupted store
- All Phase 3 commands tested via shell against the built bundle
- Tests follow existing shell test conventions (PASS/FAIL, assert_eq, summary)
- All tests pass

Edge Cases:
- Partially initialised state: directory exists but is empty (no config, no store) — not-ready
- Store file exists but is zero bytes — not-ready (MsgPack decode will fail)
- Store file exists but is a directory instead of a file — not-ready
- Config file exists but is invalid JSON — this is not a check concern (check just verifies existence). Invalid JSON is caught by config resolution (Task 3-1) when a command actually runs.
- Concurrent check while another process holds the write lock — check is read-only and does not acquire the lock, so it should proceed without blocking

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — knowledge check command (lines 673-678, exit code always 0, stdout ready/not-ready, three validation conditions), CLI command tests in Testing Strategy section (line 473, test stdout/stderr/exit codes against built knowledge.cjs)

title: OpenAI Embedding Provider
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs a real embedding provider for production use. Phase 1 created the StubProvider for testing, and Phase 3 wired provider resolution into the CLI. Now we need the OpenAI provider that generates actual semantic vectors — this is what makes the knowledge base useful beyond keyword search.

Solution: Implement an OpenAI provider that fulfils the KnowledgeProvider interface using Node's built-in fetch to call the /v1/embeddings endpoint.

Outcome: A production-ready embedding provider that drops into the existing provider resolution from Task 3-1 when config.provider is openai, enabling hybrid search with real semantic vectors.

Do:
- Create src/knowledge/providers/openai.js implementing the KnowledgeProvider interface (embed, embedBatch, dimensions) defined in Task 1-2.
- Register the provider in the provider resolution logic from Task 3-1. When config.provider is openai, instantiate OpenAIProvider with the resolved API key and configured model/dimensions.

CONSTRUCTOR:
- Accepts: apiKey (string), model (string, default text-embedding-3-small), dimensions (number, default 1536).
- The API key comes from the config resolution chain: config.api_key_env -> process.env[api_key_env] -> resolved key string. This is already handled by Task 3-1 config resolution — the provider receives the resolved key, not the env var name.

embed(text):
- POST to https://api.openai.com/v1/embeddings with:
    model: this.model
    input: text (single string)
- Headers: Authorization: Bearer {apiKey}, Content-Type: application/json
- Parse response: data[0].embedding is the vector array
- Return the vector array
- Use Node built-in fetch (no external HTTP library). Node 18+ has fetch built in. Node 18 emits ExperimentalWarning to stderr — cosmetic, not functional.

embedBatch(texts):
- Same endpoint, but input is an array of strings (the OpenAI endpoint natively accepts arrays).
- Up to 2048 inputs per request per OpenAI docs. For the knowledge base workload this limit will never be hit (typical batch is 5-15 chunks per file), but validate and chunk into multiple requests if the array exceeds 2048 items.
- Parse response: data array, each element has an embedding field. Return in the same order as input.
- This maps to one API call for multiple texts — efficient for bulk indexing.

dimensions():
- Returns this.dimensions (configured value).

ERROR HANDLING (single-layer, throw-on-failure):
- This provider throws on any failure. It does NOT retry internally. The operation-level retry wrapper from Task 4-4 is the single source of retry logic for ALL failures (including rate limits). This avoids retry compounding (3 × 3 = 9 attempts) and keeps the mental model simple.
- Network error (fetch fails): throw with clear message including the original error.
- HTTP 401 (invalid/expired API key): throw with message: OpenAI API key is invalid or expired. Check your OPENAI_API_KEY environment variable.
- HTTP 429 (rate limit): throw with clear message indicating rate limit. The operation-level retry wrapper from Task 4-4 will retry with backoff. Do NOT retry 429 inside this provider.
- HTTP 4xx/5xx (other errors): throw with status code and response body for debugging.
- The two-layer retry model (provider retry + operation retry) was considered and rejected in favour of single-layer operation retry. All retry logic lives in Task 4-4's withRetry wrapper.

Acceptance Criteria:
- embed(text) returns a float array of length dimensions()
- embedBatch([text1, text2]) returns 2 vectors in correct order
- dimensions() returns the configured value
- Provider registered in config resolution — config.provider openai with valid key creates an OpenAIProvider instance
- Invalid API key throws clear error message
- Rate limit (429) throws clear error (operation-level retry handles the actual retry)
- Network error throws clear error message
- No internal retry logic — all failures throw immediately
- npm run build succeeds with the new module

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-openai.cjs (Node test)
- Unit tests (no real API calls):
  - it constructs with correct defaults (model, dimensions)
  - it constructs with custom model and dimensions
  - it reports correct dimensions()
  - Mock fetch tests (if feasible — override global fetch in test):
    - it parses a successful embed response correctly
    - it parses a successful batch response correctly
    - it throws on 401 with descriptive message
    - it throws on 429 with rate limit message (no internal retry)
    - it throws on network error with descriptive message
- Integration test (opt-in, skipped without OPENAI_API_KEY env var):
  - Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-openai-integration.cjs
  - it embeds a single string and returns a vector of correct dimensions
  - it batch embeds multiple strings and returns correct count
  - Skip condition: if (\!process.env.OPENAI_API_KEY) { skip() }
  - This test hits the real OpenAI API. Not run in CI unless env var is set. Developer confidence only.

Edge Cases:
- Empty string input to embed — OpenAI accepts it (returns a vector). Not an error.
- Very long input — OpenAI has a token limit per input (8191 tokens for text-embedding-3-small). Chunks from the knowledge base are well under this (20-200 lines typical). If it somehow exceeds, OpenAI returns a 400 error — let it propagate with a clear message.
- embedBatch with empty array — return empty array, no API call.
- embedBatch with single item — still works (array of one).
- Concurrent embed calls — each is an independent fetch, no shared state. Thread-safe by default.
- Node 18 ExperimentalWarning for fetch — cosmetic stderr output. Does not affect functionality. Note in comments but do not suppress.
- Persistent 429 — this provider throws on first 429. The operation-level retry (Task 4-4) retries with backoff (1s, 2s, 4s). If all retries exhaust, the file goes to the pending queue. This is the correct behaviour — no compounding retry logic needed at the provider layer.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Embedding Provider section (interface definition, OpenAI default, batch embedding description at line 256), Provider evaluation table (line 247), the note about Node built-in fetch (line 453), Error Handling section (lines 756-795, retry is at operation level not provider level)

title: completed_at Manifest Migration
description:
Problem: The compact command (Task 4-5) needs to know WHEN a work unit was completed to calculate decay TTL. The manifest currently stores status: completed but no timestamp. Without completed_at, compaction cannot determine which chunks have expired.

Solution: Add a completed_at field to work unit manifests via a migration script that backfills existing completed work units using file mtime, following the project's existing migration conventions.

Outcome: All completed work units have a completed_at ISO date in their manifest, and the field is set going forward whenever a work unit is marked completed.

Do:

MIGRATION SCRIPT:
- Create skills/workflow-migrate/scripts/migrations/036-completed-at.sh (or next available number — check existing migrations first).
- The migration:
  1. Find all work unit manifest files in .workflows/*/manifest.json
  2. For each, check if status is completed and completed_at is absent
  3. If both conditions met, backfill completed_at:
     - Scan all artifact files across ALL phases in that work unit directory
     - Find the file with the latest modification time (mtime)
     - Use that mtime as the completed_at value, formatted as ISO date (YYYY-MM-DD)
  4. Write the updated manifest back
  5. Report: report_update for updated manifests, report_skip for already-having or non-completed

- CRITICAL: Migration scripts must NOT use the manifest CLI (per CLAUDE.md). Read/write manifest.json directly with node or jq. The manifest CLI validates against the current schema which changes over time — migrations are point-in-time snapshots.
- Bash 3.2 compatibility (macOS default): no mapfile, no declare -A, no local -n.
- The migration must be idempotent — running it twice produces the same result. If completed_at already exists, skip.
- Use stat for mtime. On macOS: stat -f %m (epoch seconds). On Linux: stat -c %Y. Handle both or use node for portability. The existing migration scripts may have a pattern for this — check.
- Convert epoch seconds to ISO date format (YYYY-MM-DD) for the completed_at value.

MANIFEST CLI AWARENESS:
- The manifest CLI does not need code changes for this migration — completed_at is just a field that gets set/read via the generic get/set commands.
- However, Phase 5 (Skill Integration) will need skills that set status: completed to also set completed_at: <current_date>. That is NOT this task — this task only adds the migration for backfill. Note this dependency for Phase 5 planning.

MIGRATION TEST:
- Create tests/scripts/test-migration-036.sh following existing test conventions.
- Harness structure: set -eo pipefail, PASS/FAIL counters, report_update/report_skip stubs, assert_eq function, setup/teardown with temp dir.
- Invocation: use source or bash depending on whether the migration uses return or exit (check existing test patterns).

Test cases:
- Happy path: completed work unit without completed_at gets backfilled from file mtime
- Skip: completed work unit that already has completed_at is not modified
- Skip: in-progress work unit is not modified
- Skip: cancelled work unit is not modified (cancelled work units may or may not have completed_at — the migration only targets status: completed without the field)
- Idempotent: running twice produces identical manifest content
- Multiple work units: only completed ones without completed_at are updated
- Content preservation: other manifest fields are not modified or reordered (use node -e for JSON read/write to preserve structure)

Acceptance Criteria:
- Migration script exists and runs without error on a project with mixed work unit statuses
- Completed work units without completed_at get the field backfilled from latest artifact mtime
- Completed work units with completed_at are skipped
- Non-completed work units (in-progress, cancelled) are skipped
- completed_at value is a valid ISO date (YYYY-MM-DD)
- Migration is idempotent
- Migration test passes with all test cases
- Migration is tracked in .workflows/.state/migrations (handled by the migration orchestrator, not this script)

Edge Cases:
- Completed work unit with no artifact files at all (empty directory) — skip with a warning, do not set completed_at. Compaction will treat it as having no expiry date (safe default).
- Completed work unit where all artifact files have been deleted but directory structure remains — same as above, skip.
- Work unit directory with only non-file items (subdirectories only) — stat must target files, not directories.
- Very old mtime (year 2020) — still valid as completed_at. The 6-month TTL means these chunks would expire on next compact, which is correct.
- Manifest with extra fields not in the current schema — preserve them. Read the full JSON, add completed_at, write back. Do not filter or validate other fields.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Memory Decay and Compaction section (completed_at prerequisite at lines 824-829, backfill algorithm, mtime approximation rationale), and CLAUDE.md — migration conventions (no manifest CLI in migrations, Bash 3.2 compatibility, test conventions)

title: Remove Command
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs a way to remove chunks explicitly — for work unit cancellation (remove everything), spec supersession (remove one topic's spec chunks), and spec promotion (remove chunks before relocation to a new work unit). The removal granularity must be flexible: sometimes you remove an entire work unit, sometimes just one phase, sometimes a single topic within a phase. Solution: Implement the knowledge remove command with three granularity levels controlled by flags. Outcome: A remove command that can surgically remove chunks at any granularity level without affecting unrelated chunks. Do: - Implement the remove command handler in the CLI (invoked by Task 3-1 dispatch). - Invocation: knowledge remove --work-unit [--phase

] [--topic ] - Three granularity levels (from design doc lines 651-656): 1. --work-unit only: remove ALL chunks for that work unit across all phases and topics. 2. --work-unit + --phase: remove all chunks for that work unit and phase combination. 3. --work-unit + --phase + --topic: remove chunks for that specific identity key (work_unit + phase + topic). - --work-unit is required. --phase and --topic are optional and additive (narrow the scope). - --topic without --phase is an error — topic is meaningless without a phase context. - Implementation approach: - Load the store from disk (loadStore from Task 1-4). - Acquire file lock (withLock from Task 1-4). - For granularity level 3 (all three flags): use removeByIdentity(db, { work_unit, phase, topic }) from Task 1-3. - For granularity levels 1 and 2: need a broader removal function. Options: a) Use Orama search with where clause to find all matching document IDs, then remove each. This extends the pattern from removeByIdentity. b) Add a removeByFilter(db, whereClause) function to the store module that generalises removal by any combination of enum fields. - Option (b) is cleaner — implement removeByFilter in src/knowledge/store.js and have removeByIdentity delegate to it. This keeps the store module general-purpose. - Save the store after removal. - Release lock. - Output: summary of what was removed. Removed {N} chunks for {work_unit} (all phases) Removed {N} chunks for {work_unit}/{phase} Removed {N} chunks for {work_unit}/{phase}/{topic} - If no chunks match: Removed 0 chunks for {description} — not an error, just a no-op. This is important for idempotent operations (e.g., cancelling a work unit that was never indexed). - Rebuild after implementation: npm run build. Acceptance Criteria: - knowledge remove --work-unit auth-flow removes all chunks for auth-flow - knowledge remove --work-unit auth-flow --phase discussion removes only discussion chunks for auth-flow - knowledge remove --work-unit auth-flow --phase specification --topic auth-flow removes only that topic's spec chunks - Non-matching chunks are unaffected at all granularity levels - Removing from an epic with 5 spec topics using --topic only removes the targeted topic - No chunks match: outputs 0 removed, exits cleanly - --topic without --phase produces clear error - Missing --work-unit produces clear error - Store is saved after removal - File lock acquired during operation Tests: - Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (extend CLI shell tests) - Setup: index multiple fixture files across different work units, phases, and topics. - it removes all chunks for a work unit - it removes chunks for a work unit + phase combination - it removes chunks for a specific work unit + phase + topic - it does not affect chunks from other work units - it does not affect other topics when removing one topic in an epic - it reports 0 removed when no chunks match - it errors when --topic is given without --phase - it errors when --work-unit is missing Edge Cases: - Remove from empty store — 0 removed, no error - Remove work unit that was never indexed — 0 removed, no error - Remove after partial indexing (some phases indexed, some not) — only indexed phases have chunks to remove - Epic with multiple spec topics: remove --phase specification --topic auth-flow must leave other topic specs intact. This is the granularity test from the design doc line 656. - Concurrent remove while another process is indexing — file lock prevents corruption Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — knowledge remove command (lines 651-656, three granularity levels, epic topic granularity example), Work Unit Lifecycle section (cancelled work units auto-removed), Staleness and Supersession section (superseded spec chunks removed, promoted spec chunks removed from original)

title: Bulk Index + Retry/Pending Queue
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs two operational features that work together. First, a bulk indexing mode that discovers all completed artifacts and indexes any that are missing — used by knowledge setup and for manual catch-up. Second, a retry mechanism with a pending queue so that transient failures (API timeouts, rate limits, network errors) do not permanently lose files — the system self-heals on the next successful operation.

Solution: Implement the no-args form of knowledge index (bulk discovery + indexing) and a single-layer retry/pending queue mechanism that wraps both single-file indexing AND query operations.

Outcome: Running knowledge index with no arguments discovers and indexes all missing completed artifacts. Failures are retried with backoff, and persistently failing files are queued for catch-up on the next successful invocation. Query failures also retry before surfacing errors.

Do:

BULK INDEX (extend the index command from Task 3-3):
- Invocation: knowledge index (no arguments — the presence/absence of a file argument determines single vs bulk mode)
- Discovery algorithm (from design doc lines 479-484):
    1. Get all work units via the manifest CLI: list all work unit names from the project manifest.
    2. For each work unit, get completed items across indexed phases (research, discussion, investigation, specification) via manifest CLI get commands.
    3. For each completed item, resolve the file path via manifest resolve (Task 3-2).
    4. Check which items are already in the store — query for chunks matching each work_unit + phase + topic combination. If chunks exist, the item is already indexed.
    5. Index the missing ones using the single-file index logic from Task 3-3.
- Output progress as files are indexed: Indexing {file}... {N} chunks
- Final summary: Indexed {total_new} files ({total_chunks} chunks). {skipped} already indexed.
- Acquire file lock for the entire bulk operation (not per-file — avoids repeated lock/unlock overhead).

RETRY MECHANISM (single layer — all retry lives here):
- Implement a general-purpose retry wrapper: withRetry(fn, { maxAttempts: 3, backoff: [1000, 2000, 4000] }) -> result or throw.
- This wraps any async operation — not specific to indexing. It is used for:
    1. Single-file index operations (update Task 3-3 to use it)
    2. Bulk index per-file operations
    3. Query embed operations (update Task 3-4 query command to wrap the provider.embed call)
- The design doc specifies retry on BOTH indexing and query failures (lines 756-776). The retry wrapper is general enough for both.
- THIS IS THE ONLY RETRY LAYER. Task 4-1's OpenAI provider does NOT retry internally — it throws on any failure (including 429 rate limits). All retry/backoff logic lives in this wrapper. This avoids retry compounding (the previous two-layer design could have produced 3 × 3 = 9 API calls for a rate-limited request).
- The wrapper handles all transient failures uniformly: 429 (rate limit), network errors, timeouts, etc. The backoff [1s, 2s, 4s] is appropriate for rate limit recovery and transient network issues.
- For query failures specifically (design doc lines 769-776): after 3 retries exhausted, the CLI should exit with a non-zero code and a clear error message. The skill instructions (Phase 6) handle pausing the workflow and offering the user the choice to proceed without context. The CLI just retries and then fails clearly.

PENDING QUEUE:
- Location: the pending array in .workflows/.knowledge/metadata.json (schema established in Task 1-4, initialised on first index per Task 3-3).
- When all 3 retries are exhausted for an INDEX operation:
    - Log it to the pending queue: { file: <path>, failed_at: <ISO timestamp>, error: <error message> }
    - Output a clear message: Failed to index {file} after 3 attempts: {error}. Added to pending queue.
    - Do NOT stop the bulk index — continue with remaining files.
- The phase itself is not blocked — the artifact file is saved, just not indexed yet.
- Query failures do NOT go to the pending queue — they are transient operations, not files to be tracked.

CATCH-UP MECHANISM:
- After a successful single-file index (knowledge index <file>), process up to 5 items from the pending queue.
- Each pending item gets its own fresh 3-retry budget (independent of previous failures).
- Items that fail again stay in the queue for the next cycle.
- Items that succeed are removed from the queue.
- This provides gradual self-healing through normal workflow use without bursty API calls after long outages.
- knowledge index (no args — bulk mode) processes the entire pending queue alongside missing files (no 5-item limit in bulk mode).

Acceptance Criteria:
- knowledge index (no args) discovers all completed artifacts across all work units
- Already-indexed artifacts are skipped
- Missing artifacts are indexed
- Progress output shows each file being indexed
- Summary output shows totals (new, skipped)
- Retry: transient failure on first attempt succeeds on retry (for both index and query)
- Retry: rate limit (429) from OpenAI is retried by this wrapper (provider does not retry)
- Retry: 3 failures on index logs file to pending queue with error message
- Retry: 3 failures on query exits with non-zero code and clear error (no pending queue entry)
- Pending queue: failed file appears in metadata.json pending array
- Catch-up: next successful single-file index processes up to 5 pending items
- Catch-up: successfully caught-up items are removed from pending queue
- Catch-up: items that fail again remain in pending queue
- Bulk mode processes entire pending queue (no 5-item limit)
- Single-file index from Phase 3 (Task 3-3) now uses the retry wrapper
- Query command from Phase 3 (Task 3-4) now uses the retry wrapper for embed calls
- No compounding retry behaviour — exactly 3 retry attempts total per operation, not 3 × 3 = 9

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (extend CLI shell tests)
- Bulk index tests:
  - it discovers and indexes all missing completed artifacts
  - it skips already-indexed artifacts
  - it reports progress and summary
- Retry tests (may need a mock or controlled failure mechanism):
  - it retries on transient failure and succeeds
  - it retries on 429 rate limit (provider throws, wrapper catches and retries)
  - it logs to pending queue after 3 index failures
  - it exits with error after 3 query failures (no pending queue entry)
- Catch-up tests:
  - it processes pending items after a successful single-file index
  - it removes successfully caught-up items from pending queue
  - it leaves still-failing items in pending queue
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-retry.cjs (Node test for retry logic unit tests)
  - it retries up to 3 times with backoff
  - it succeeds on second attempt if first fails
  - it throws after 3 failures
  - it records correct backoff delays
  - it does not compound retry attempts (exactly 3 max per operation)

Edge Cases:
- Bulk index on a project with no completed work units — 0 files to index, clean exit
- Bulk index on a project with no .workflows/ directory — error (knowledge check should have caught this, but handle gracefully)
- Pending queue has items from a previous provider that no longer matches config — provider mismatch detection (from Task 3-3) will refuse these. They stay in the queue until the user runs knowledge rebuild.
- Pending queue grows very large (many failures over time) — catch-up processes 5 at a time in single-file mode, preventing bursty API usage. Bulk mode clears the entire backlog.
- Catch-up item file no longer exists (deleted since failure) — remove from pending queue with a warning, do not error.
- Concurrent bulk index — file lock prevents corruption. Second invocation waits or times out.
- Persistent rate limiting — after 3 backoff attempts (1s + 2s + 4s = 7s total), the operation fails. For indexing, it goes to the pending queue for catch-up. For query, it exits with a clear error. No further API calls until the user acts.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — knowledge index no-args description (lines 639-642), Discovery algorithm (lines 479-484), Error Handling section (lines 756-795, BOTH indexing AND query failure handling, retry mechanism, pending queue, catch-up), Catch-Up Mechanism section (pending queue schema in metadata.json, 5-item limit per single-file invocation)

title: Compact Command
description:
Problem: The knowledge base grows over the project lifetime. Old research, discussions, and investigations become less valuable as their decisions move into specs and code. Without compaction, the index grows without bound, increasing load times and memory usage. Compaction implements the memory decay model from the design doc — removing expired non-spec chunks while preserving specs forever.

Solution: Implement the knowledge compact command that applies the decay TTL to remove expired chunks from completed work units.

Outcome: A compact command that keeps the active index lean by removing stale non-spec chunks, with a dry-run mode for previewing what would be removed.

Do:
- Implement the compact command handler in the CLI (invoked by Task 3-1 dispatch).
- Invocation: knowledge compact [--dry-run]

DECAY RULES (from design doc lines 833-840):
- In-progress work units: ALL chunks kept regardless of age. No completed_at date means nothing can expire.
- Completed work units: specification chunks kept FOREVER (never compacted). Research, discussion, and investigation chunks removed when completed_at + decay_months <= now (inclusive).
- Cancelled work units: should already be removed by the remove command during cancellation (Phase 5). But if any remain, compact should not touch them — cancellation removal is a separate lifecycle action.
- decay_months comes from the merged config (Task 3-1). Default is 6. Set to false to disable compaction entirely (keep everything). Set to 0 for immediate expiry of non-spec chunks.

MANIFEST ACCESS PATTERN:
- The compact command reads work unit status and completed_at from the manifest. It MUST use the manifest CLI (manifest.cjs) for this — do NOT read .workflows/{work_unit}/manifest.json directly.
- Rationale: the manifest CLI is the single source of truth for manifest state (per CLAUDE.md). Direct file reads would duplicate path conventions and bypass validation. The manifest CLI precedent is established in Task 3-2 (resolve command used by Task 4-4 bulk index) and Task 5-2 (manifest.cjs set for completed_at writes).
- Specifically: shell out to the manifest CLI using get commands of the form: node .claude/skills/workflow-manifest/scripts/manifest.cjs get WORK_UNIT status, and similarly for completed_at. Collect both values in one manifest read where possible, or cache per-work_unit to avoid repeated shells.
- Shelling out has a small cost per work unit; with compacted scale (500-2K chunks, 10-50 work units), this is negligible.

ALGORITHM:
1. Load the store from disk.
2. Read decay_months from config. If false, output Compaction disabled and exit.
3. Calculate the cutoff date: now minus decay_months.
4. For each unique work_unit in the store:
   a. Shell out to manifest.cjs to get the work unit status and completed_at fields.
   b. If status is not completed, skip (in-progress work units are exempt).
   c. If completed_at is absent, skip (cannot calculate expiry — the migration from Task 4-2 should have set this, but be defensive).
   d. If completed_at is after the cutoff date, skip (not yet expired).
   e. If expired: find all chunks for this work unit where phase is NOT specification. These are the candidates for removal. Track which phases contributed chunks for each work unit (needed for the output summary).
5. If --dry-run: output what would be removed without removing. Exit.
6. Otherwise: remove the candidate chunks. Save the store. Update metadata.json.

OUTPUT (from design doc lines 863-869):
- When chunks are removed:
    Compacted: removed N chunks from M work units (completed > decay_months months ago)
      - work_unit: n chunks (research, discussion)
      - work_unit: n chunks (investigation)
- The phase list in parentheses is the unique set of phases that had chunks removed for that work unit (accumulated during step 4e).
- When nothing expires: no output (silent skip).
- Dry-run: same format but prefixed with [dry-run] and no actual removal.

- Acquire file lock during write (not during dry-run — dry-run is read-only).
- Rebuild after implementation: npm run build.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Compact uses manifest.cjs (not direct manifest.json reads) to look up status and completed_at
- Compact removes non-spec chunks from work units completed more than decay_months ago
- Spec chunks are NEVER removed regardless of age
- In-progress work unit chunks are never removed
- Chunks from work units completed within the TTL window are kept
- --dry-run shows what would be removed without removing
- decay_months: false disables compaction entirely
- decay_months: 0 expires all non-spec chunks from completed work units immediately
- Missing completed_at on a completed work unit is handled gracefully (skip with no error)
- Output format matches the design doc summary style with phase accumulation per work unit
- Nothing to compact: silent exit (no output)
- Store is saved after removal
- File lock acquired during write, not during dry-run

Tests:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (extend CLI shell tests)
- Setup: index fixtures across multiple work units with different completion dates. Set some as completed with old completed_at values, others recent, others in-progress. Use manifest.cjs set to populate status and completed_at.
- it removes expired non-spec chunks from old completed work units
- it preserves spec chunks from expired work units
- it preserves all chunks from in-progress work units
- it preserves all chunks from recently completed work units (within TTL)
- it shows removal plan in --dry-run without removing
- it handles decay_months: false (compaction disabled)
- it handles decay_months: 0 (immediate expiry)
- it handles missing completed_at gracefully
- it outputs summary in correct format with phase breakdown
- it produces no output when nothing to compact
- it uses manifest.cjs for status/completed_at lookups (can be verified by test setup using manifest.cjs set)

Edge Cases:
- Work unit completed exactly at the cutoff boundary — inclusive per design doc line 837: completed_at + decay_months <= now means expired.
- Config has no decay_months field — use default (6).
- Store has chunks from a work unit that no longer has a manifest (work unit directory deleted manually) — manifest.cjs get will fail; catch the error and skip with no error. The status command (Task 4-6) reports these as orphans.
- All chunks for a work unit are specs — nothing to remove, work unit not mentioned in summary.
- Compact runs but store has no chunks at all — silent exit.
- Multiple work units sharing the same cutoff — each is processed independently.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Memory Decay and Compaction section (lines 808-898, decay model table, compaction rules, output format, configuration), and the compaction trigger description (lines 847-855, Step 0 sequence in skills). Manifest CLI precedent established in Task 3-2 (resolve) and Task 5-2 (set completed_at).

title: Status + Rebuild + Batch Queries + Phase 4 CLI Tests
description:
Problem: Three remaining CLI features need implementation: status (health reporting for debugging), rebuild (destructive reindex for provider changes), and batch queries (multiple search terms in one invocation). These are all Phase 4 completeness items — the knowledge base is functional without them but they round out the CLI for production use.

Solution: Implement the status, rebuild, and batch query features, plus a comprehensive CLI test suite covering all Phase 4 commands.

Outcome: A complete CLI with all 8 commands implemented, plus tests validating everything works against the built bundle.

Do:

STATUS COMMAND (from design doc lines 680-688 + review findings 11-13):
- Invocation: knowledge status (no arguments)
- Full health report — not used in skill automation, intended for user/debugging.
- Report sections:
    1. Index summary: total chunk count, broken down by work unit, phase, and work type.
    2. Last indexed: timestamp of most recent indexing operation (from metadata.json last_indexed).
    3. Store health: store file size on disk, provider name + model + dimensions from metadata.json.
    4. Pending items: count and list from metadata.json pending array. Show file path and failure reason for each.
    5. Mode indicator: Full (hybrid search) or Keyword-only (stub mode) based on whether a provider is configured.
    6. Provider mismatch warning: if metadata.json provider/model/dimensions differs from current config, warn: Config has changed since last index. Run knowledge rebuild to reindex.
    7. Orphan detection (finding #11): chunks in the store whose source_file no longer exists on disk. Report count and list.
    8. Unindexed artifacts (finding #11): completed artifacts found via manifest that are not in the store. Report count and list. Uses the same discovery logic as bulk index (Task 4-4) but only reports, does not index.
    9. Manifest-knowledge consistency (finding #12): cross-reference manifest state against store contents. Report:
       - Superseded specs still indexed: specs whose manifest status is superseded but chunks still exist in the store (should have been removed by the supersession action in Phase 5).
       - Promoted specs not relocated: specs whose manifest status is promoted but chunks still exist under the original work unit (should have been removed and re-indexed under the new cross-cutting work unit).
       - Cancelled work units still present: chunks for work units whose manifest status is cancelled (should have been removed by the cancellation action in Phase 5).
       These are consistency warnings — the processing skills handle the operations, status catches failures.
    10. Stub-to-full upgrade note (finding #13): if config has a provider with API key but store was built in keyword-only mode, note: Keyword-only mode but embedding provider configured. Run knowledge rebuild for full hybrid search.
- Output is plain text, human-readable. Not JSON, not machine-parsed.
- Read-only — no file lock needed, no store modifications.

REBUILD COMMAND (from design doc lines 644-649):
- Invocation: knowledge rebuild
- Destructive reindex from current artifacts. Drops existing index, re-chunks and re-embeds everything.
- HUMAN-ONLY — uses interactive confirmation prompts via process.stdin. Use a type-the-action-word pattern (like Terraform destroy or GitHub repo delete) rather than yes/no — it forces engagement with what is being done:
    Warning: This will delete the existing index and rebuild from scratch.
    This is non-deterministic — the rebuilt index will differ from the original.
    Type 'rebuild' to confirm:
- If confirmation does not match the word exactly, abort.
- If confirmed: delete store.msp, delete metadata.json, then run bulk index logic from Task 4-4 (knowledge index no-args internally).
- Claude cannot handle interactive terminals — this is the natural protection against accidental invocation.

BATCH QUERIES (extend the query command from Task 3-4):
- Invocation: knowledge query <term1> <term2> [<termN>...] [--flags]
- Multiple positional arguments = multiple search terms.
- Implementation (from design doc line 602-603):
    1. Load the store once.
    2. Run a separate search for each term (embed + search + re-rank).
    3. Merge all result sets.
    4. Deduplicate by chunk ID — if the same chunk appears in multiple result sets, keep the highest score.
    5. Sort merged results by score (descending).
    6. Apply --limit to the merged set (not per-query).
    7. Format output as normal (same provenance format from Task 3-4).
- This is efficient: one store load, multiple searches, one formatted output.

STUB-TO-FULL UPGRADE NOTE ON QUERY OUTPUT (finding #13, line 992):
- When the store was built in keyword-only mode but the current config has a provider with API key configured, prepend a one-line upgrade note to the query output (before the result count header):
    [keyword-only mode but embedding provider configured — run knowledge rebuild for full hybrid search]
- This is separate from the existing stub mode note (which appears when NO provider is configured). The stub-to-full note specifically flags the upgrade opportunity when a provider HAS been configured but the store has not been rebuilt yet.
- Applies to both single and batch query invocations.
- Detection: compare metadata.json (what the store was built with) against the current resolved config (what the system is configured for now). If current config has a provider + API key but metadata.json indicates keyword-only, show the note.
- Design doc rationale: this surfaces the upgrade opportunity naturally during workflow use, not just on manual status checks.

PHASE 4 CLI TESTS:
- Test file: tests/scripts/test-knowledge-cli.sh (extend with Phase 4 command tests)
- Status tests:
  - it reports chunk counts by work unit and phase
  - it reports store size and provider info
  - it reports pending queue items
  - it warns about provider mismatch
  - it detects orphaned chunks (source file deleted)
  - it detects unindexed completed artifacts
  - it reports superseded specs still indexed
  - it reports cancelled work units still present
  - it reports keyword-only mode when no provider
  - it reports stub-to-full upgrade note when provider available but store is keyword-only
- Rebuild tests:
  - it prompts for confirmation (test by piping non-matching input — should abort)
  - it aborts when confirmation does not match the word rebuild
  - (full rebuild is hard to test non-interactively — test the abort path, note the confirm path for manual testing)
- Batch query tests:
  - it returns merged results from multiple search terms
  - it deduplicates chunks appearing in multiple result sets (keeps highest score)
  - it applies --limit to the merged set
  - it handles batch where one term has results and another does not
- Stub-to-full query note tests:
  - it shows the upgrade note on query output when config has provider but store is keyword-only
  - it does NOT show the upgrade note when store and config match
  - it does NOT show the upgrade note in pure stub mode (no provider configured)

Acceptance Criteria:
- knowledge status outputs a comprehensive health report with all 10 sections
- Status correctly detects orphaned chunks and unindexed artifacts
- Status correctly warns about provider mismatch
- Status correctly reports superseded specs still indexed, cancelled work units still present (manifest-knowledge consistency)
- knowledge rebuild prompts with type-the-word confirmation and aborts on mismatch
- knowledge rebuild (when confirmed manually) deletes and recreates the index
- Batch query merges and deduplicates results from multiple terms
- Batch query applies limit to the merged result set
- Stub-to-full upgrade note appears on knowledge query output when applicable
- All Phase 4 CLI tests pass

Edge Cases:
- Status on empty store — reports 0 chunks, no orphans, may report unindexed artifacts
- Status when metadata.json is missing — report what is available, note missing metadata
- Status when no manifest exists (no .workflows/) — report error for manifest-dependent checks, still report store-only metrics
- Rebuild when store does not exist — nothing to delete, proceed with bulk index
- Batch query with duplicate terms (same term twice) — should work, deduplication handles it
- Batch query with one term that matches nothing — merged results only contain results from the other term(s)
- Rebuild in non-interactive environment (piped stdin) — read from stdin for the type-the-word confirmation. If stdin is not a TTY and no input is provided, abort (do not hang).
- Manifest-knowledge consistency when manifest has been updated but skills have not run yet (e.g., spec manually marked superseded) — status reports the inconsistency, user runs knowledge remove manually
- Stub-to-full note on query output during a store just transitioned — user ran setup with a new provider but has not rebuilt yet. Note appears until rebuild.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — knowledge status command (lines 680-688), knowledge rebuild command (lines 644-649), batch query description (line 602-603), Index-artifact drift detection review finding #11 (line 985), Manifest-knowledge consistency review finding #12 (line 988, superseded/promoted/cancelled checks), Stub-to-full upgrade detection review finding #13 (line 991-992, including the note on query output)

title: Hard Stop Reference + Step 0 Integration
description:
Problem: Entry-point skills need to gate on knowledge base readiness before proceeding. Without this gate, skills would attempt to query or index against a non-existent knowledge base. The design doc makes the knowledge base required infrastructure — no graceful degradation, just a clear hard stop with setup instructions.

Solution: Create a hard stop reference file and update Step 0 in all 11 entry-point skills to run knowledge check after migrations, display the hard stop if not ready, and run knowledge compact if ready.

Outcome: Every workflow invocation checks knowledge base readiness before proceeding, with a clear user-facing message when setup is needed, and automatic compaction keeping the index lean.

Do:

HARD STOP REFERENCE FILE:
- Create skills/workflow-shared/references/knowledge-not-ready.md (or similar shared location — check where other shared references live).
- This file is loaded and displayed when knowledge check returns not-ready.
- Content should follow the existing hard stop / terminal condition conventions from CLAUDE.md:
    - Phase title box explaining what happened
    - Signpost blockquote explaining why the knowledge base is needed
    - Clear instruction: run knowledge setup to initialise
    - The exact command: node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs setup
    - Brief explanation of what setup does (system config, project init, initial indexing)
    - Mention stub mode is available if no API key
    - Terminal stop — do not proceed
- Follow the display conventions from CLAUDE.md: phase title (bullet-bordered box), signpost blockquote, rendering instructions.

STEP 0 CHANGES:
- Update Step 0 in ALL 11 entry-point skills. The affected files:
    skills/start-feature/SKILL.md
    skills/start-epic/SKILL.md
    skills/start-bugfix/SKILL.md
    skills/start-quickfix/SKILL.md
    skills/start-cross-cutting/SKILL.md
    skills/continue-feature/SKILL.md
    skills/continue-epic/SKILL.md
    skills/continue-bugfix/SKILL.md
    skills/continue-quickfix/SKILL.md
    skills/continue-cross-cutting/SKILL.md
    skills/workflow-start/SKILL.md

- The Step 0 sequence after this change:
    1. Migrations (existing — preserve as-is)
    2. Knowledge check: run node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs check and capture stdout.
       - If stdout is not-ready: load the hard stop reference file and follow its instructions. STOP — do not proceed.
       - If stdout is ready: proceed.
    3. Knowledge compact: run node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs compact (no flags — not dry-run). This is silent when nothing to compact. Output only appears if chunks were removed.

- The existing Step 0 structure varies between start-* and continue-* skills:
    - start-* skills CHECK if /workflow-migrate has been invoked (they expect it was already run)
    - continue-* and workflow-start ACTIVELY run migrations if not done
  The knowledge check + compact goes AFTER the migration handling in both cases. Read each skill's Step 0 to understand its current structure before modifying.

- Each skill's Step 0 will need its own rendering instruction for the check result / compact output if applicable. Follow the existing conventions in each skill.

ALLOWED-TOOLS:
- Add Bash(node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs) to the allowed-tools frontmatter of all 11 entry-point skills.
- This follows the same pattern as the manifest CLI allowed-tools entries already in these files. Check one skill first to see the exact format.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Hard stop reference file exists and follows display conventions
- All 11 entry-point skills have knowledge check in Step 0 after migrations
- knowledge check returning not-ready loads the hard stop reference and stops
- knowledge check returning ready proceeds normally
- All 11 entry-point skills run knowledge compact after successful check
- Compact output is visible when chunks are removed, silent when nothing to compact
- allowed-tools frontmatter updated in all 11 entry-point skills
- Existing migration handling in Step 0 is preserved and unmodified
- The Step 0 ordering is: migrations -> knowledge check -> knowledge compact

Tests:
- No automated tests for this task — it is a skill file editing task. Validation is manual:
  - Invoke a start-* skill with no knowledge base set up -> verify hard stop appears
  - Invoke a continue-* skill with knowledge base set up -> verify check passes and compact runs
  - Verify allowed-tools entries are present in all 11 files

Edge Cases:
- knowledge check fails with a genuine error (not not-ready, but a crash) — the skill should surface the error, not silently proceed. Check exit code: 0 is expected (ready or not-ready on stdout). Non-zero means something broke.
- knowledge compact fails (e.g., store locked) — should surface the error but not hard-stop the workflow. Compaction failure is a warning, not a blocker. The knowledge base still works, just with stale chunks.
- Existing Step 0 in start-* skills only checks if migrations were invoked — adding knowledge steps must not change this conditional structure. The check and compact are unconditional additions after the migration section.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Knowledge Base is Required Infrastructure section (lines 304-320, setup flow, hard stop), Compaction Trigger section (lines 847-855, Step 0 sequence: migrations -> check -> compact), allowed-tools frontmatter changes (lines 728-733)

title: Phase Completion Indexing
description:
Problem: The knowledge base needs to grow automatically as workflow phases complete. Without automatic indexing at phase completion, users would need to manually run knowledge index after every discussion, research session, investigation, or specification — which would never happen consistently.

Solution: Add knowledge index <file> calls at the phase completion points in processing skills for all indexed phases, plus scoping (which produces a spec). Also audit the skills/ directory for every place that transitions a work unit to status: completed and add completed_at alongside each one.

Outcome: Every completed workflow artifact is automatically indexed into the knowledge base, and every work unit completion path sets completed_at for the compact command's TTL calculation.

Do:
- Add knowledge index <file> at the phase completion step in each of these processing skills:

    skills/workflow-research-process/SKILL.md
    skills/workflow-discussion-process/SKILL.md
    skills/workflow-investigation-process/SKILL.md
    skills/workflow-specification-process/SKILL.md
    skills/workflow-scoping-process/SKILL.md

- For each skill, find the step where the phase artifact is finalised and committed. The index call goes AFTER the artifact is saved to disk and committed, but BEFORE any phase transition or handoff to the next phase. The artifact must exist on disk before indexing.

- The index call pattern:
    Run: node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs index <artifact_path>
  Where <artifact_path> is the path to the completed artifact file. Each phase has a different path convention:
    - Research: .workflows/{work_unit}/research/{topic}.md (may vary — use the actual file that was just written)
    - Discussion: .workflows/{work_unit}/discussion/{topic}.md
    - Investigation: .workflows/{work_unit}/investigation/{topic}.md
    - Specification: .workflows/{work_unit}/specification/{topic}/specification.md
    - Scoping: produces a specification — index the spec file it creates, NOT a scoping artifact (scoping is not an indexed phase per design doc)

- The index call should reference the actual file path variable/value used in the skill, not a hardcoded path. Read each skill to understand how it references the artifact file.

- FAILURE HANDLING: indexing failure must NOT block the phase. The artifact is already saved. If knowledge index fails, output the error message (which includes retry-failed + pending queue info from Task 4-4) and continue with the phase transition. The design doc is explicit: the phase itself is not blocked — the artifact is saved. But the failure is made visible, not silently swallowed.

- MULTIPLE INDEXED ARTIFACTS: some skills may complete multiple artifacts. For example, scoping produces both a specification and potentially sets up planning. Only the spec needs indexing. Each index call is independent — a failure on one does not block the other (per design doc line 707).

COMPLETED_AT AUDIT (concrete instruction, not judgement call):
- The completed_at field (added by the migration in Task 4-2) needs to be set going forward whenever ANY code path transitions a work unit to status: completed. Missing a path means the work unit will never expire under compaction (Task 4-5 handles missing completed_at defensively, but the result is silent non-compaction).
- AUDIT STEP: grep the skills/ directory for every place that sets status to completed. Suggested grep patterns:
    grep -rn 'status.*completed' skills/
    grep -rn 'manifest.cjs set.*status.*completed' skills/
    grep -rn 'completed' skills/ | grep -i status
- For EACH match, add a manifest.cjs set {work_unit} completed_at {current_date_ISO} call alongside the status-setting call. Format: YYYY-MM-DD (ISO date, same as the migration backfill in Task 4-2).
- Known locations (verified to exist — the grep is still the authoritative source, but these are the high-traffic files):
    - skills/workflow-bridge/references/ — 5 continuation files set status: completed (bugfix-continuation.md, feature-continuation.md, epic-continuation.md, cross-cutting-continuation.md, quickfix-continuation.md). Bridge is where most feature/bugfix/quickfix completions happen — do not miss these.
    - The 5 processing skills listed above (research, discussion, investigation, specification, scoping) — conclude-*.md references
    - skills/workflow-review-process/references/review-actions-loop.md — review is often the last phase and may mark the work unit completed
    - skills/workflow-implementation-process/references/conclude-implementation.md — implementation completion
    - skills/workflow-specification-process/references/spec-completion.md and promote-to-cross-cutting.md — specification lifecycle
    - skills/workflow-scoping-process/references/write-tasks.md and write-specification.md — scoping completion paths
    - skills/workflow-start/references/manage-work-unit.md — the manage menu done action, covered by Task 5-3
- DO NOT rely on the list above being exhaustive. The grep is the authoritative source of truth. Add completed_at setting wherever the grep finds status: completed being set, even if the file is not in the list above.
- Report the full list of files modified (for review) when completing this task.
- Acceptance post-condition: after editing, grep -rn 'status.*completed' skills/ should show every match adjacent (same step or reference block) to a corresponding completed_at set. This is a mechanical check the implementer can run to self-verify coverage.

- Add allowed-tools for knowledge.cjs to these 5 processing skills (if not already added by Task 5-1 or Task 5-3).

Acceptance Criteria:
- Research process skill indexes the research artifact on completion
- Discussion process skill indexes the discussion artifact on completion
- Investigation process skill indexes the investigation artifact on completion
- Specification process skill indexes the specification artifact on completion
- Scoping process skill indexes the spec it produces on completion
- Index failure does not block the phase — error is displayed, phase continues
- Every place in skills/ that sets status: completed also sets completed_at (verified via grep audit)
- All 5 bridge continuation files are updated (bugfix, feature, epic, cross-cutting, quickfix)
- The list of files modified for completed_at is documented in the task completion report
- Post-condition grep shows every status: completed match adjacent to a completed_at set
- allowed-tools updated in all 5 processing skills

Tests:
- No automated tests — skill file editing task. Validation is manual:
  - Complete a discussion in a test project -> verify chunks appear in knowledge base (knowledge status shows them)
  - Complete a specification -> verify chunks appear
  - Simulate index failure (e.g., corrupt store) -> verify phase still completes, error is shown
  - Grep skills/ after task completion: every status-completed line should have a neighbouring completed_at line

Edge Cases:
- Scoping produces a spec but scoping itself is not an indexed phase — only the spec output is indexed. The index call must reference the spec file path, not a scoping artifact path.
- Research in epics can produce multiple files — each file may need separate indexing. Check how the research process skill handles multiple outputs. If it produces one file per research topic, each gets its own index call.
- completed_at audit finds a skill that doesn't import the manifest CLI — check the skill's allowed-tools and add if needed
- Phase completion with no artifact file (edge case — should not happen in normal flow) — knowledge index will error on missing file, which is correct and visible.
- A skill sets status: completed on a DIFFERENT work unit than its own (e.g., the manage menu) — this is covered by Task 5-3 for the manage menu specifically.
- Bridge continuation files may set completed on a SOURCE work unit when transitioning (e.g., a feature completion closing out the feature before bridging to the next). Ensure completed_at is set on the same work unit whose status is being set.

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Indexing section (lines 337-341, primary trigger is phase completion, not mid-phase), Integration with Workflow Skills section (lines 707-708, indexing at phase completion, independent per artifact, failure does not block), completed_at field (line 824-829, set explicitly by skill at completion time going forward, the manage menu done action and any skill that sets status: completed must also set completed_at)

title: Lifecycle Removal (Cancellation + Supersession/Promotion)
description:
Problem: When work units are cancelled or specs are superseded/promoted, their chunks must be removed from the knowledge base. Without this, stale and contradictory information pollutes search results. Additionally, the manage menu's done action needs to set completed_at for compaction TTL calculation.

Solution: Add knowledge remove calls to the manage menu reference (cancellation) and specification process (supersession/promotion) skills. Add completed_at setting to the manage menu's done action.

Outcome: Work unit cancellation and spec lifecycle changes automatically clean the knowledge base, and completed work units have the timestamp needed for decay compaction.

Do:

CANCELLATION REMOVAL (manage menu reference):
- The manage menu lives inside the workflow-start skill at skills/workflow-start/references/manage-work-unit.md (NOT a separate workflow-manage skill — verified).
- Locate the cancellation action in that file (where status is set to cancelled).
- Add inline: node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs remove --work-unit {work_unit}
- This runs INLINE as part of the cancellation action — no prompt, cancelling means done with it (design doc line 801).
- If removal fails (store locked, CLI error): log the error visibly but do not block the cancellation. The pending queue is designed for indexing failures, not removal failures. If removal fails, output the error and note that the user can run knowledge remove manually.
- NO frontmatter edit is needed here — skills/workflow-start is an entry-point skill that already receives allowed-tools for knowledge.cjs in Task 5-1. The reference file inherits from its parent skill.

COMPLETED_AT ON DONE ACTION (manage menu reference):
- Locate the done/completion action in skills/workflow-start/references/manage-work-unit.md (where status is set to completed).
- Add: manifest.cjs set {work_unit} completed_at {current_date_ISO}
- The completed_at field is what compaction (Task 4-5) uses to calculate decay TTL. Without it, completed work units cannot expire and the index grows without bound.
- This complements Task 5-2 which adds completed_at in processing skills and bridge continuations where completion happens automatically. The manage menu is where users manually mark work units as done — both paths must set the field.
- Format: YYYY-MM-DD (ISO date, same as the migration backfill in Task 4-2).

SUPERSESSION REMOVAL (specification process skill):
- Find the specification process skill: skills/workflow-specification-process/SKILL.md
- Locate the supersession action — where a spec's manifest status changes to superseded.
- Add: node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs remove --work-unit {work_unit} --phase specification --topic {topic}
- This removes ONLY the superseded topic's spec chunks. Other topics in the same work unit (e.g., other specs in an epic) are unaffected. Granularity matters (design doc line 656).
- The specification process skill already has allowed-tools for knowledge.cjs from Task 5-2 (for indexing). No additional frontmatter change needed.

PROMOTION REMOVAL (specification process skill):
- Locate the promotion action — where a spec's manifest status changes to promoted.
- Add the same remove command: node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs remove --work-unit {work_unit} --phase specification --topic {topic}
- Promotion is a RELOCATION, not a deletion (design doc line 535). The chunks are removed from the original work unit. The new cross-cutting spec will be indexed naturally when it completes in the new work unit. There is a timing gap where the content is absent from the knowledge base — this is accepted per design doc line 537.
- Research, discussion, and investigation chunks from the original work unit are KEPT — only the spec chunks are removed (design doc line 539).

ALLOWED-TOOLS SUMMARY FOR PHASE 5:
- Task 5-1 adds to 11 entry-point skills (for check + compact). workflow-start is one of them, which covers the manage-work-unit.md reference file used by this task.
- Task 5-2 adds to 5 indexed phase processing skills (for index), and updates bridge continuation files.
- This task adds NO new allowed-tools entries — all target files are covered by Tasks 5-1 or 5-2.
- Total Phase 5 allowed-tools changes: 16 skills (11 from 5-1 + 5 from 5-2).
- The 3 non-indexed processing skills (planning, implementation, review) get allowed-tools in Phase 6 Task 6-2 when autonomous querying is added — no Phase 5 reason to add them now.
- Phase entry skills (workflow-*-entry) do NOT get allowed-tools in Phase 5. The one exception is workflow-planning-entry, which gets allowed-tools in Phase 6 Task 6-3 specifically for the cross-cutting query. No other phase entry skill needs it.

Acceptance Criteria:
- Cancelling a work unit via manage menu removes its chunks from the knowledge base
- Cancellation removal failure does not block the cancellation action
- Manage menu done action sets completed_at on the work unit manifest
- Superseding a spec removes only that topic's spec chunks
- Promoting a spec removes only that topic's spec chunks from the original work unit
- Research/discussion/investigation chunks from the same work unit survive supersession and promotion
- No new allowed-tools frontmatter edits in this task (covered by Task 5-1 for workflow-start, Task 5-2 for specification-process)

Tests:
- No automated tests — skill file editing task. Validation is manual:
  - Cancel a work unit that has indexed chunks -> verify chunks are removed (knowledge status)
  - Mark a work unit as done via manage menu -> verify completed_at is set in manifest
  - Supersede a spec topic in an epic -> verify only that topic's spec chunks are removed, other topics and non-spec phases survive

Edge Cases:
- Cancellation of a work unit that was never indexed — knowledge remove returns 0 removed, no error. The cancellation flow should not treat this as a failure.
- Supersession of a spec that was never indexed — same, 0 removed, no error.
- Promotion timing gap — between removing original spec chunks and the new cross-cutting spec completing, the content is absent from the knowledge base. This is accepted per design doc.
- Epic with multiple spec topics — superseding one MUST NOT remove others. The --topic flag ensures granularity.
- Done action on a work unit that already has completed_at — overwrite with current date (the user is explicitly marking it done now, regardless of previous state).

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Work Unit Lifecycle section (lines 799-801, cancelled work units auto-removed, failure handling), Staleness and Supersession section (lines 530-539, superseded removed, promoted relocated, research/discussion/investigation kept), completed_at prerequisite (line 824, manage menu done action must set it), allowed-tools frontmatter changes (lines 728-733)

title: Knowledge SKILL.md + Two-Step Retrieval Documentation
description:
Problem: The knowledge base CLI exists (Phases 1-4) and is wired into skills (Phase 5), but Claude has no documentation explaining what it is, how to use it, or when to query it. Without Layer 1, Layers 2 and 3 have nothing to reference for API details.

Solution: Create the knowledge skill file (SKILL.md) that serves as the API documentation layer — what commands exist, how to call them, what the output looks like, and how the two-step retrieval pattern works.

Outcome: A comprehensive skill file that any processing skill can reference for knowledge base API details, enabling Claude to query the knowledge base correctly.

Do:
- Create skills/workflow-knowledge/SKILL.md following the project's skill file conventions (see CLAUDE.md Skill File Structure section).
- This is a model-invocable skill, not user-invocable. It is loaded by reference from Layer 2 usage references (Task 6-2). It is NOT directly invoked by users.
- Content sections:

  PURPOSE:
  - What the knowledge base is: a RAG system storing all workflow artifacts at full fidelity for semantic search
  - Why it exists: cross-work-unit and intra-work-unit context surfacing
  - What it indexes: research, discussion, investigation, specification (not planning, implementation, review)

  QUERY COMMAND:
  - Single query: node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs query <search_term> [flags]
  - Batch query: node .claude/skills/workflow-knowledge/scripts/knowledge.cjs query <term1> <term2> [termN...] [flags]
    Multiple positional arguments run separate searches, merge results, deduplicate by chunk ID (highest score kept), apply --limit to merged set. Efficient: one store load, multiple searches.
  - Flags: --work-type, --phase, --work-unit (re-ranking hint, not filter), --limit
  - Search modes: hybrid (default when provider configured), keyword-only (when no provider)
  - Query construction guidance: use natural language, not topic slugs. Descriptive queries produce better semantic matching. Multiple queries from different angles are encouraged.
  - Example queries showing good vs poor query construction

  OUTPUT FORMAT:
  - Document the exact format (from design doc lines 580-603):
      [N results]
      [phase | work_unit/topic | confidence | YYYY-MM-DD]
      chunk content verbatim
      Source: source_file_path
  - Result count header, provenance line, verbatim content, source path
  - Empty results: [0 results]
  - Stub mode note when applicable: [keyword-only mode — configure embedding provider for semantic search]

  CONFIDENCE TIERS:
  - Explain what confidence means and how to weigh it:
      specification (high) — validated decisions, what was decided and why
      investigation (medium) — diagnostic knowledge, tied to specific symptoms
      discussion (low-medium) — conversational, may contain corrected assumptions
      research (low) — exploratory, may contain dead ends and rejected paths
  - Low confidence is not low value — research that rejected an approach prevents re-exploring dead ends

  TWO-STEP RETRIEVAL PATTERN:
  - Step 1: query returns chunks with provenance (lightweight, lands in context)
  - Step 2: if a chunk looks relevant, read the actual source file (source_file in metadata) for full detail
  - This keeps context lean — surface-level awareness, deep dive on demand
  - Claude should NOT read source files for every result, only when a chunk warrants deeper investigation

  WHAT NOT TO DO:
  - Do not dump large result sets into context speculatively
  - Do not query using topic slugs as search terms (poor semantic signal)
  - Do not query during specification phase (explicitly excluded — spec turns decisions into a golden doc, cross-cutting concerns merge at planning time)
  - Do not prepend metadata to stored content (already in enum fields)

Acceptance Criteria:
- SKILL.md exists at skills/workflow-knowledge/SKILL.md
- Documents both single and batch query invocation forms
- Documents all query flags and their behaviour
- Documents the exact output format with examples
- Documents confidence tiers with guidance on how to weigh them
- Documents the two-step retrieval pattern (query -> read source file on demand)
- Documents what NOT to do (spec exclusion, slug queries, speculative dumps)
- Follows project skill file conventions from CLAUDE.md
- Can be loaded by reference from Layer 2 files (Task 6-2)

Tests:
- No automated tests — documentation task. Validation is manual:
  - Read the SKILL.md and verify it provides enough information to correctly construct and interpret knowledge queries
  - Verify it follows the project's skill file structure conventions

Edge Cases:
- The SKILL.md must describe BOTH hybrid and keyword-only modes without making keyword-only feel broken — it is a supported degraded mode
- Must not duplicate the CLI help text verbatim — this is higher-level guidance about when and why, not just how
- Must explain that --work-unit is a re-ranking hint, not a filter — this is a common misunderstanding that would produce wrong queries if not clear
- Batch query section must explain deduplication and merged limit behaviour

Spec Reference: knowledge-base/design.md — Three-layer integration pattern (lines 716-724, Layer 1 description), Retrieved Context Format (lines 576-605, exact output format), Confidence Tiers (lines 514-525), Two-step retrieval pattern (lines 735-737), Query construction (line 737), Batch query (line 602-603, merged and deduplicated), Specification excluded from automatic retrieval (line 560)

title: Per-Phase Usage References + Contextual Query at Phase Start
description:
Problem: Layer 1 (SKILL.md) documents the API, but Claude needs phase-specific guidance on WHEN to query and WHY — the trigger heuristics contextualised for each phase's work. Without Layer 2, Claude has the capability but not the judgment to use it effectively. Additionally, Claude needs explicit guidance on how to handle query failures — the design doc mandates pausing the workflow, not silently proceeding.
